title.zh: 卷六十二 司馬遷傳
title.raw: 卷六十二 司馬遷傳 Volume 62: Sima Qian

sentences.zh: 卷六十二 司馬遷傳第三十二

translations.literal: Volume 62: Biographies the Thirty-second, Sima Qian.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 62: Biography 32—Sima Qian.

sentences.zh: 昔在顓頊，命南正重司天，火正黎司地。

translations.literal: Of old under Zhuanxu, he ordered the south rectifier Chong to administer heaven and the fire rectifier Li to administer earth.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhuanxu's day the throne assigned Chong the south rectifier to watch the heavens and Li the fire rectifier to watch the earth.

sentences.zh: 唐、虞之際，紹重、黎之後，使復典之，至於夏、商，故重、黎氏世序天地。

translations.literal: At the transition of Tang and Yu, he continued Chong and Li's posterity, caused them again to preside over it, down to Xia and Shang; therefore the Chong and Li clans for generations ordered heaven and earth.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yao and Shun the lines of Chong and Li were restored to that charge, and through Xia and Shang their houses kept the ordering of heaven and earth.

sentences.zh: 其在周，程伯林甫其後也。

translations.literal: As for their being in Zhou, Marquis of Cheng Linfu was their descendant.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhou times their descendant was Cheng Bo Linfu.

sentences.zh: 當宣王時，官失其守而為司馬氏。

translations.literal: In King Xuan's time the office lost its charge and became the Sima clan.
translations.idiomatic: Under King Xuan the old portfolio lapsed, and the family took the surname Sima.

sentences.zh: 司馬氏世典周史。

translations.literal: The Sima clan for generations presided over Zhou's annals.
translations.idiomatic: For generations the Simas kept Zhou's archives.

sentences.zh: 惠、襄之間，司馬氏適晉。

translations.literal: Between Hui and Xiang, the Sima clan went to Jin.
translations.idiomatic: Between Kings Hui and Xiang the family moved to Jin.

sentences.zh: 晉中軍隨會奔魏，而司馬氏入少梁。

translations.literal: Jin's central army commander Shi Hui fled to Wei, and the Sima clan entered Shaoliang.
translations.idiomatic: When central commander Shi Hui of Jin fled to Wei, the Simas settled in Shaoliang.

sentences.zh: 自司馬氏去周適晉，分散，或在衛，或在趙，或在秦。

translations.literal: From the time the Sima clan left Zhou for Jin, they scattered; some were in Wei, some in Zhao, some in Qin.
translations.idiomatic: After the Simas left Zhou for Jin the line split among Wei, Zhao, and Qin.

sentences.zh: 其在衛者，相中山。

translations.literal: Those who were in Wei became chancellor of Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: The Wei branch produced a chancellor of Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 在趙者，以傳劍論顯，蒯聵其後也。

translations.literal: Those in Zhao became eminent through treatises on sword transmission; Kuai Kui was their descendant.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhao line rose on fencing theory; Kuai Kui was of that stock.

sentences.zh: 在秦者錯，與張儀爭論，於是惠王使錯將兵伐蜀，遂拔，因而守之。

translations.literal: The one in Qin was Cuo; he debated with Zhang Yi; thereupon King Hui sent Cuo to lead troops to attack Shu, then took it, and thereupon guarded it.
translations.idiomatic: In Qin, Cuo crossed swords in debate with Zhang Yi; King Hui sent him to conquer Shu, which he held thereafter.

sentences.zh: 錯孫蘄，事武安君白起。

translations.literal: Cuo's grandson was Qi; he served Lord Wu'an Bai Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Cuo's grandson Qi served Lord Wu'an, Bai Qi.

sentences.zh: 而少梁更名夏陽。

translations.literal: And Shaoliang was renamed Xiayang.
translations.idiomatic: Shaoliang was later renamed Xiayang.

sentences.zh: 蘄與武安君坑趙長平軍，還而與之俱賜死杜郵，葬於華池。

translations.literal: Qi together with Lord Wu'an buried alive Zhao's army at Changping; returning, together with him he was ordered to die at Duyou and was buried at Huachi.
translations.idiomatic: Qi helped Bai Qi slaughter the Zhao army at Changping; on the march home both were ordered to commit suicide at Duyou and were buried at Huachi.

sentences.zh: 蘄孫昌，為秦王鐵官。

translations.literal: Qi's grandson was Chang; he was Qin's king's ironworks officer.
translations.idiomatic: Qi's grandson Chang ran the royal foundries for the king of Qin.

sentences.zh: 當始皇之時，蒯聵玄孫卬為武信君將而徇朝歌。

translations.literal: In First Emperor's time Kuai Kui's great-great-grandson Ang was general under Lord Wuxin and campaigned to Zhaoge.
translations.idiomatic: Under the First Emperor, Ang—fourth-generation descendant of Kuai Kui—served Lord Wuxin as a general and reduced Zhaoge.

sentences.zh: 諸侯之相王，王卬於殷。

translations.literal: When the feudal lords set one another up as kings, they made Ang king in Yin.
translations.idiomatic: When the lords began proclaiming kings, Ang was enthroned in the Yin region.

sentences.zh: 漢之伐楚，卬歸漢，以其地為河內郡。

translations.literal: In Han's attack on Chu, Ang submitted to Han; his territory became Henei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: When Han struck Chu, Ang came over, and his lands were organized as Henei commandery.

sentences.zh: 昌生毋懌，毋懌為漢市長。

translations.literal: Chang begat Wu Yi; Wu Yi was Han's market chief.
translations.idiomatic: Chang's son Wu Yi became market commissioner under Han.

sentences.zh: 毋懌生喜，喜為五大夫，卒，皆葬高門。

translations.literal: Wu Yi begat Xi; Xi was a fifth-rank grandee; when he died, both were buried at Gaomen.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Yi's son Xi held fifth-rank grandee rank; father and son lie buried at Gaomen.

sentences.zh: 喜生談，談為太史公。

translations.literal: Xi begat Tan; Tan was the grand scribe.
translations.idiomatic: Xi fathered Tan, who became grand scribe.

sentences.zh: 太史公學天官於唐都，受《易》於楊何，習道論於黃子。

translations.literal: The grand scribe studied celestial offices with Tang Du, received the Changes from Yang He, and practiced Daoist discourse with Master Huang.
translations.idiomatic: The grand scribe studied astronomy under Tang Du, the Changes under Yang He, and Daoist theory under Master Huang.

sentences.zh: 太史公仕於建元、元封之間，愍學者不達其意而師悖，乃論六家之要指曰：

translations.literal: The grand scribe served between Jianyuan and Yuanfeng; pitying scholars who did not grasp the meaning and whose teachers were perverse, he thereupon discussed the essential points of the six schools, saying:
translations.idiomatic: While serving between Jianyuan and Yuanfeng, the grand scribe grieved that students misunderstood the masters and took up confused teachers, and set out the gist of the six schools:

sentences.zh: 《易大傳》：「天下一致而百慮，同歸而殊塗。」

translations.literal: The Great Commentary on the Changes: "All under heaven has one goal but a hundred thoughts; it has one return but many paths."
translations.idiomatic: The Great Commentary says: "The empire shares one end though worries divide it; all roads lead home though routes differ."

sentences.zh: 夫陰陽、儒、墨、名、法、道德，此務為治者也。

translations.literal: As for Yin-Yang, Ru, Mo, Ming, Fa, and Dao-De—these are devoted to good order.
translations.idiomatic: Yin-Yang, Confucian, Mohist, Sophist, Legalist, and Daoist teachings all aim at governing well.

sentences.zh: 直所從言之異路，有省不省耳。

translations.literal: Only the paths from which they speak differ; there is insight and lack of insight, that is all.
translations.idiomatic: They differ only in how they argue the case—some see clearly, some do not.

sentences.zh: 嘗竊觀陰陽之術，大詳而眾忌諱，使人拘而多畏，然其敘四時之大順，不可失也。

translations.literal: I have privately observed the arts of Yin-Yang: very detailed and with many taboos, making men constrained and much afraid; yet in their ordering the great compliance of the four seasons, it cannot be lost.
translations.idiomatic: The Yin-Yang school piles detail and taboos until men shrink in fear, yet its calendar of the seasons must not be ignored.

sentences.zh: 儒者博而寡要，勞而少功，是以其事難盡從，然其敘君臣、父子之禮，列夫婦、長幼之別，不可易也。

translations.literal: The Ru are broad yet grasp few essentials, toil yet achieve little merit; therefore their affairs are hard to follow in full; yet in their ordering the rites of lord and minister, father and son, and arraying the distinctions of husband and wife, elder and younger, it cannot be altered.
translations.idiomatic: Confucians cast a wide net for thin gain—hard to follow wholesale—yet their map of ruler and subject, father and son, husband and wife, old and young cannot be set aside.

sentences.zh: 墨者儉而難遵，是以其事不可偏循；

translations.literal: The Moists are frugal yet hard to comply with; therefore their affairs cannot be wholly followed;
translations.idiomatic: Mohism is austere to the point of impracticality—not a program you can follow to the letter;

sentences.zh: 然其強本節用，不可廢也。

translations.literal: yet in their strengthening the root and restraining expenditure, it cannot be discarded.
translations.idiomatic: yet their stress on fundamentals and thrift remains indispensable.

sentences.zh: 法家嚴而少恩，然其正君臣上下之分，不可改也。

translations.literal: The Legalists are severe and scant in kindness; yet in their rectifying the divisions of superior and inferior between lord and minister, it cannot be changed.
translations.idiomatic: Legalism is harsh and thin on mercy, yet its fix on hierarchy between throne and subject is not to be tampered with.

sentences.zh: 名家使人儉而善失真，然其正名實，不可不察也。

translations.literal: The Sophists make men constrained and apt to lose the genuine; yet in their rectifying names and realities, it cannot but be examined.
translations.idiomatic: The Sophists tie men in knots and blur truth, yet their discipline of matching names to things demands attention.

sentences.zh: 道家使人精神專一，動合無形，澹足萬物。

translations.literal: The Daoists make men's spirit and essence single and unified; movement matches the formless; they are placidly sufficient for the myriad things.
translations.idiomatic: Daoism gathers spirit into one, moves with the unseen pattern, and lets the myriad things find sufficiency in stillness.

sentences.zh: 其為術也，因陰陽之大順，采儒、墨之善，撮名、法之要，與時遷徙，應物變化，立俗施事，無所不宜，指約而易操，事少而功多。

translations.literal: As for their art, they follow Yin-Yang's great compliance, take Ru and Mo's good points, gather Ming and Fa's essentials, shift with the times, respond to things' changes, establish custom and carry out affairs—nothing is unsuitable; the aim is concise and easy to grasp, affairs are few yet merit is much.
translations.idiomatic: Their method rides the seasons' flow, borrows what is sound in Confucianism and Mohism, distills Legalist and Sophist essentials, moves with the times, answers each change, sets custom and policy with ease—few moves, great effect.

sentences.zh: 儒者則不然，以為人主天下之儀表也，君唱臣和，主先臣隨。

translations.literal: The Ru are not so; they deem the human lord the model for all under heaven: the ruler leads the chant and ministers harmonize, the lord goes first and ministers follow.
translations.idiomatic: Confucians read it differently: the ruler is the world's exemplar—he leads, ministers echo; he steps first, they trail.

sentences.zh: 如此，則主勞而臣佚。

translations.literal: In this case the lord toils and ministers are at ease.
translations.idiomatic: That leaves the sovereign worn out while his ministers rest easy.

sentences.zh: 至於大道之要，去健羨，黜聰明，釋此而任術。

translations.literal: As for the essential of the great Dao, discard vigor and covetousness, dismiss keen hearing and sight, release this and trust in method.
translations.idiomatic: The great Dao's heart is to shed force and greed, dim cleverness, drop such burdens, and rule by method.

sentences.zh: 夫神大用則竭，形大勞則敝；

translations.literal: When spirit is greatly used it is exhausted; when form is greatly toiled it is worn;
translations.idiomatic: Spirit spent to the limit runs dry; the body driven without rest breaks down;

sentences.zh: 神形蚤衰，欲與天地長久，非所聞也。

translations.literal: spirit and form decay early; desiring to be long-lasting with heaven and earth—this is not what I have heard of.
translations.idiomatic: premature decay of body and soul is no path to matching heaven and earth in endurance.

sentences.zh: 夫陰陽，四時、八位、十二度、二十四節各有孝令，曰「順之者昌，逆之者亡」，未必然也，故曰「使人拘而多畏」。

translations.literal: As for Yin-Yang, the four seasons, eight positions, twelve degrees, and twenty-four nodes each have their injunctions, saying "those who comply flourish, those who oppose perish"—this is not necessarily so; therefore it is said "it makes men constrained and much afraid."
translations.idiomatic: Yin-Yang heaps seasonal rules, eight stations, twelve divisions, twenty-four solar terms—each with its command that compliance brings fortune and defiance doom—which is not always true; hence "it binds men in fear."

sentences.zh: 夫春生、夏長、秋收、冬藏，此天道之大經也，弗順，則無以為天下紀綱。

translations.literal: Spring's growth, summer's flourishing, autumn's harvest, winter's storing—this is the great warp of heaven's way; if you do not comply, there is no way to be the cords and net for all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Spring sprouting, summer growth, autumn reaping, winter storing—that is heaven's main thread; ignore it and you cannot order the realm.

sentences.zh: 故曰「四時之大順，不可失也」。

translations.literal: Therefore it is said "the great compliance of the four seasons cannot be lost."
translations.idiomatic: Hence "the seasons' great order must never be neglected."

sentences.zh: 夫儒者，以六藝為法，六藝經傳以千萬數，累世不能通其學，當年不能究其禮。

translations.literal: As for the Ru, they take the six arts as their model; the six arts' classics and commentaries number in the thousands and myriads; piled generations cannot exhaust their learning, a single lifetime cannot exhaust their rites.
translations.idiomatic: Confucians take the six arts as law; commentaries run to millions of words—generations cannot master them, nor one life their ritual detail.

sentences.zh: 故曰「博而寡要，勞而少功」。

translations.literal: Therefore it is said "broad yet grasping few essentials, toiling yet achieving little merit."
translations.idiomatic: Hence "wide learning, thin gist; heavy labor, small yield."

sentences.zh: 若夫列君臣、父子之禮，序夫婦、長幼之別，雖百家弗能易也。

translations.literal: As for arraying the rites of lord and minister, father and son, and ordering the distinctions of husband and wife, elder and younger—even the hundred schools cannot alter this.
translations.idiomatic: Yet for ordering ruler and subject, father and son, husband and wife, elder and child, no school can improve on them.

sentences.zh: 墨者亦上堯、舜，言其德行，曰「堂高三尺，土階三等，茅茨不剪，采椽不斫；

translations.literal: The Moists also exalt Yao and Shun, speaking of their virtue and conduct, saying "the hall is three chi high, earthen steps in three ranks, thatched roof not trimmed, plain rafters not adzed;
translations.idiomatic: Mohists too revere Yao and Shun, citing their ways: "halls three feet high, three earthen steps, unshorn thatch, unplaned rafters;

sentences.zh: 飯土簋，歠土刑，糲梁之食，藜藿之羹；

translations.literal: they eat from earthen gui, drink from earthen xing, coarse millet for food, chenopodium broth;
translations.idiomatic: meals from clay bowls, drink from clay cups, coarse grain, wild-greens soup;

sentences.zh: 夏日葛衣，冬日鹿裘。」

translations.literal: in summer kudzu cloth, in winter deerskin robes."
translations.idiomatic: ramie in summer, deerskin in winter."

sentences.zh: 其送死，桐棺三寸，舉音不盡其哀。

translations.literal: In sending off the dead, paulownia coffin three cun thick; raised voices do not exhaust their grief.
translations.idiomatic: For the dead they prescribe a three-inch paulownia coffin and mourning that stops short of full wail.

sentences.zh: 教喪禮，必以此為萬民率。

translations.literal: Teaching funeral rites, they must take this as the model for the myriad people.
translations.idiomatic: They would make this the universal standard for burial rites.

sentences.zh: 故天下法若此，則尊卑無別也。

translations.literal: Therefore if all under heaven modeled on this, then high and low would have no distinction.
translations.idiomatic: Were the realm to follow that rule, high and low could not be told apart.

sentences.zh: 夫世異時移，事業不必同，故曰「儉而難遵」也。

translations.literal: Generations differ and times shift; undertakings need not be the same; therefore it is said "frugal yet hard to comply with."
translations.idiomatic: Times change and institutions with them—hence "austere and hard to live by."

sentences.zh: 要曰「強本節用」，則人給家足之道也。

translations.literal: In sum, as for "strengthening the root and restraining expenditure," this is the way to men supplied and households sufficient.
translations.idiomatic: Yet "fortify the root and curb spending" is the road to plenty for every household.

sentences.zh: 此墨子之所長，雖百家不能廢也。

translations.literal: This is where Mozi excels; even the hundred schools cannot discard it.
translations.idiomatic: That is Mozi's strength, and no rival school may discard it.

sentences.zh: 法家不別親疏，不殊貴賤，一斷於法，則親親尊尊之恩絕矣，可以行一時之計，而不可長用也，故曰「嚴而少恩」。

translations.literal: The Legalists do not distinguish near and distant kin, do not differentiate noble and base; one judgment by law—then the kindness of loving kin and honoring superiors is cut off; it can carry out a moment's plan but cannot be long used; therefore it is said "severe and scant in kindness."
translations.idiomatic: Legalists cut kin and rank alike under one code, severing the bonds of family and deference—useful for a short fix, not for the long haul—hence "harsh and thin in mercy."

sentences.zh: 若尊主卑臣，明分職不得相逾越，雖百家不能改也。

translations.literal: As for honoring the lord and demeaning ministers, clarifying offices so none may overstep—even the hundred schools cannot change this.
translations.idiomatic: Yet for exalting the ruler, humbling ministers, and fixing duties so none oversteps, no school offers a better frame.

sentences.zh: 名家苛察繳繞，使人不得反其意，剸決於名，時失人情，故曰「使人儉而善失真」。

translations.literal: The Sophists are harsh in scrutiny and tangled in coils, making men unable to return to their meaning; they chop judgment by names and often miss human feeling; therefore it is said "they make men constrained and apt to lose the genuine."
translations.idiomatic: Sophists tie argument in knots until meaning is lost and feeling is bruised—hence "constricting and prone to falsify reality."

sentences.zh: 若夫控名責實，參伍不失，此不可不察也。

translations.literal: As for matching names and demanding realities, cross-checking so nothing is lost—this cannot but be examined.
translations.idiomatic: Yet holding names to facts and cross-checking evidence is something no ruler can ignore.

sentences.zh: 道家無為，又曰無不為，其實易行，其辭難知。

translations.literal: The Daoist school is non-active, and again says there is nothing it does not do; its substance is easy to practice, its phrasing hard to know.
translations.idiomatic: Daoism teaches nonaction yet claims nothing is left undone; the practice is simple, the words obscure.

sentences.zh: 其術以虛無為本，以因循為用。

translations.literal: Its art takes emptiness and nothingness as root, takes following along as use.
translations.idiomatic: It roots in emptiness and works through yielding.

sentences.zh: 無成勢，無常形，故能究萬物之情。

translations.literal: It has no fixed configuration, no constant form; therefore it can exhaust the dispositions of the myriad things.
translations.idiomatic: Without fixed stance or shape, it can sound every thing's nature.

sentences.zh: 不為物先後，故能為萬物主。

translations.literal: It does not for things go first or after; therefore it can be master of the myriad things.
translations.idiomatic: It neither leads nor trails events, and so commands them all.

sentences.zh: 有法無法，因時為業；

translations.literal: There is law and there is no law; according to the times is the task;
translations.idiomatic: Sometimes rule, sometimes leave off—each age sets the task;

sentences.zh: 有度無度，因物興捨。

translations.literal: there is measure and there is no measure; according to things rise or set aside.
translations.idiomatic: sometimes measure, sometimes not—things themselves tell you when to build or drop.

sentences.zh: 故曰「聖人不巧，時變是守」。

translations.literal: Therefore it is said "the sage is not clever; guarding the shifts of the times is his task."
translations.idiomatic: Hence "the sage wields no cleverness; he only keeps pace with change."

sentences.zh: 虛者，道之常也；

translations.literal: Emptiness is the Dao's constant;
translations.idiomatic: Emptiness is the constant of the Way;

sentences.zh: 因者，君之綱也。

translations.literal: following along is the ruler's guideline.
translations.idiomatic: yielding is the ruler's guideline.

sentences.zh: 群臣並至，使各自明也。

translations.literal: The host of ministers all arrive; he causes each to clarify himself.
translations.idiomatic: When ministers all come forward, each must prove his own case.

sentences.zh: 其實中其聲者謂之端，實不中其聲者謂之款。

translations.literal: When the reality matches the name it is called correct; when the reality does not match the name it is called hollow.
translations.idiomatic: Fact that fits the claim is straight; fact that fails the claim is false.

sentences.zh: 款言不聽，奸乃不生，賢不肖自分，白黑乃形。

translations.literal: Hollow words are not heeded, treachery then does not arise, worthy and unworthy each distinguish themselves, white and black then take form.
translations.idiomatic: Reject empty speech and intrigue dies; worth and worthlessness sort themselves; truth and falsehood show plain.

sentences.zh: 在所欲用耳，何事不成！

translations.literal: It lies in what you wish to employ—what affair will not succeed!
translations.idiomatic: Use men as they deserve—what then will not succeed?

sentences.zh: 乃合大道，混混冥冥。

translations.literal: Then it matches the great Dao, vast and dark.
translations.idiomatic: Thus it joins the great Dao, dark and boundless.

sentences.zh: 光耀天下，復反無名。

translations.literal: Its radiance overspreads all under heaven, then returns again to the nameless.
translations.idiomatic: Its light fills the realm, then sinks back into the nameless.

sentences.zh: 凡人所生者神也，所托者形也。

translations.literal: What all men are born with is spirit; what they rely on is form.
translations.idiomatic: Men are born of spirit and housed in body.

sentences.zh: 神大用則竭，形大勞則敝，形神離則死。

translations.literal: When spirit is greatly used it is exhausted; when form is greatly toiled it is worn; when form and spirit part there is death.
translations.idiomatic: Overwork the spirit and it fails; overwork the body and it breaks; part body from spirit and you die.

sentences.zh: 死者不可復生，離者不可復合，故聖人重之。

translations.literal: The dead cannot live again; the parted cannot join again; therefore the sage esteems this.
translations.idiomatic: The dead do not return, nor severed spirit and flesh rejoin—hence the sage guards both.

sentences.zh: 由此觀之，神者生之本，形者生之俱。

translations.literal: Viewed from this, spirit is life's root, form is life's tool.
translations.idiomatic: From this we see: spirit is the root of life, the body its instrument.

sentences.zh: 不先定其神形，而曰「我有以治天下」，何由哉？

translations.literal: If you do not first settle their spirit and form, yet say "I have means to order all under heaven"—by what path?
translations.idiomatic: Without first ordering your own spirit and body, how can you claim to govern the realm?

sentences.zh: 太史公既掌天官，不治民。

translations.literal: The grand scribe having taken charge of celestial offices, did not govern the people.
translations.idiomatic: The grand scribe held the astronomy portfolio and did not administer commoners.

sentences.zh: 有子曰遷。

translations.literal: He had a son named Qian.
translations.idiomatic: He had a son, Qian.

sentences.zh: 遷生龍門，耕牧河山之陽。

translations.literal: Qian was born at Dragon Gate, plowing and herding on the south of the river mountains.
translations.idiomatic: Qian was born at Longmen and worked the south bank fields and pastures of the Yellow River.

sentences.zh: 年十歲則誦古文。

translations.literal: At age ten he then recited ancient script texts.
translations.idiomatic: At ten he was already chanting archaic texts.

sentences.zh: 二十而南遊江、淮，上會稽，探禹穴，窺九疑，浮沅、湘。

translations.literal: At twenty he traveled south on the Yangzi and Huai, ascended Kuaiji, explored Yu's cavern, peered into Jiuyi, floated on Yuan and Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: At twenty he toured the Yangzi and Huai, climbed Kuaiji, sought Yu's cave, gazed toward Jiuyi, and sailed the Yuan and Xiang.

sentences.zh: 北涉汶、泗，講業齊魯之都，觀夫子遺風，鄉射鄒嶧；

translations.literal: North he forded Wen and Si, lectured on learning in Qi and Lu's capitals, observed the Master's surviving airs, and did village archery at Zou and Yi;
translations.idiomatic: He crossed north to Wen and Si, studied in the capitals of Qi and Lu, caught the lingering tone of the Master, and witnessed the village archery rites at Zou and Yi;

sentences.zh: 厄困蕃、薛、彭城，過梁、楚以歸。

translations.literal: he was in straits at Fan, Xue, and Pengcheng, passed through Liang and Chu and returned.
translations.idiomatic: he fell on hard times in Fan, Xue, and Pengcheng, then came home by way of Liang and Chu.

sentences.zh: 於是遷仕為郎中，奉使西征巴、蜀以南，略邛、莋、昆明，還報命。

translations.literal: Thereupon Qian served as gentleman of the palace; bearing orders he campaigned west of Ba and Shu to the south, reduced Qiong, Zuo, and Kunming, returned and reported completion.
translations.idiomatic: He then entered service as a gentleman of the palace and was sent west of Ba and Shu to reconnoiter Qiong, Zuo, and Kunming before reporting back.

sentences.zh: 是歲，天子始建漢家之封，而太史公留滯周南，不得與從事，發憤且卒。

translations.literal: That year the Son of Heaven first established Han's feng sacrifice, while the grand scribe was detained south of Zhou and could not join in the affair; he vented his resentment and was about to die.
translations.idiomatic: That year the emperor first performed the Han feng rite on Mount Tai; the grand scribe, stuck south of the Zhou heartland, could not attend, nursed his grief, and neared death.

sentences.zh: 而子遷適反，見父於河、洛之間。

translations.literal: And his son Qian happened to return and saw his father between the Yellow and Luo rivers.
translations.idiomatic: His son Qian returned just then and met his father between the Yellow and Luo.

sentences.zh: 太史公執遷手而泣曰：「予先，周室之太史也。

translations.literal: The grand scribe grasped Qian's hand and wept, saying, "My ancestors were Zhou's grand scribes.
translations.idiomatic: The grand scribe took Qian's hand in tears: "Our forebears were grand scribes of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 自上世嘗顯功名虞、夏，典天官事。

translations.literal: From high antiquity they once displayed merit and fame in Yu and Xia, presiding over celestial offices.
translations.idiomatic: Since remote ages they won name under Yu and Xia in charge of heaven's offices.

sentences.zh: 後世中衰，絕於予乎？

translations.literal: Later generations declined midway—will it end with me?
translations.idiomatic: Later lines faded—must the thread break with me?

sentences.zh: 汝復為太史，則續吾祖矣。

translations.literal: If you again become grand scribe, then you continue my ancestors.
translations.idiomatic: If you take the grand scribe's post, you continue our ancestors' work.

sentences.zh: 今天子接千歲之統，封泰山，而予不得從行，是命也夫！

translations.literal: Now the Son of Heaven receives the thread of a thousand years, fengs Mount Tai, yet I cannot follow in the march—this is fate, is it not!
translations.idiomatic: The emperor now inherits a millennial mandate and performs feng on Tai, yet I cannot go—such is fate!

sentences.zh: 命也夫！

translations.literal: Fate, is it not!
translations.idiomatic: Fate indeed!

sentences.zh: 予死，爾必為太史；

translations.literal: When I die, you must become grand scribe;
translations.idiomatic: When I die, you must become grand scribe;

sentences.zh: 為太史，毋忘吾所欲論著矣。

translations.literal: when you are grand scribe, do not forget what I wished to discuss and write.
translations.idiomatic: and never forget the work I meant to write.

sentences.zh: 且夫孝，始於事親，中於事君，終於立身；

translations.literal: Moreover as for filial piety, it begins in serving parents, reaches fulfillment in serving the lord, ends in establishing the person;
translations.idiomatic: Filial duty begins in serving parents, rises through serving the ruler, and ends in establishing your own character;

sentences.zh: 揚名於後世，以顯父母，此孝之大也。

translations.literal: to raise one's name for later ages, thereby honoring father and mother—this is filial piety's greatness.
translations.idiomatic: to win a name for later ages and bring honor to your parents is the crown of filial piety.

sentences.zh: 夫天下稱周公，言其能論歌文、武之德，宣周、召之風，達大王、王季思慮，爰及公劉，以尊後稷也。

translations.literal: As for all under heaven praising the Duke of Zhou, they say he could discuss and sing Wen and Wu's virtue, spread the Zhou and Shao airs, penetrate Great King and Wang Ji's thoughts, extend to Gong Liu, thereby honoring Hou Ji.
translations.idiomatic: The world praises the Duke of Zhou for hymning the virtue of Wen and Wu, spreading the airs of Zhou and Shao, tracing the intent of Great King and Wang Ji back through Gong Liu to glorify Hou Ji.

sentences.zh: 幽、厲之後，王道缺，禮樂衰，孔子修舊起廢，論《詩》、《書》，作《春秋》，則學者至今則之。

translations.literal: After You and Li, the kingly way was lacking, rites and music declined; Confucius repaired the old and raised the fallen, discussed the Odes and Documents, composed the Spring and Autumn, and scholars to the present take it as their model.
translations.idiomatic: After Kings You and Li the royal way cracked and ritual music faded; Confucius restored the broken canon, taught the Odes and Documents, and wrote the Spring and Autumn—still the scholar's polestar.

sentences.zh: 自獲麟以來四百有餘歲，而諸侯相兼，史記放絕。

translations.literal: From the capture of the unicorn onward over four hundred years, while the feudal lords swallowed one another, scribal records were scattered and cut off.
translations.idiomatic: More than four hundred years have passed since the unicorn hunt, ages of annexation among the lords, and the annals lie in ruins.

sentences.zh: 今漢興，海內一統，明主賢君，忠臣義士，予為太史而不論載，廢天下之文，予甚懼焉，爾其念哉！」

translations.literal: Now Han has risen, within the seas unified, enlightened lords and worthy kings, loyal ministers and righteous knights—if I as grand scribe do not discuss and record them, I discard the writings of all under heaven; I am greatly afraid of this—you must heed it!"
translations.idiomatic: Han now rules a single realm—clear-sighted sovereigns, loyal ministers, gallant men—yet if I, grand scribe, leave them unwritten I would silence the age itself; I dread that—remember!"

sentences.zh: 遷俯首流涕曰：「小子不敏，請悉論先人所次舊聞，不敢闕。」

translations.literal: Qian bowed his head, weeping, and said, "Your young servant is not keen; I beg to discuss in full what my forbears arranged of old hearsay—I dare not omit."
translations.idiomatic: Qian bowed in tears: "I am dull, yet I will set down everything my father ordered from tradition—nothing shall be left out."

sentences.zh: 卒三歲，而遷為太史令，紬史記石室金鐀之書。

translations.literal: Three years after he died, Qian became grand scribe director and drew out from the stone chamber and metal-chested books the scribal records.
translations.idiomatic: Three years later Qian was appointed grand scribe director and began combing the archives locked in stone vaults and metal chests.

sentences.zh: 五年而當太初元年，十一月甲子朔旦冬至，天歷始改，建於明堂，諸神受記。

translations.literal: In the fifth year it fell on the first year of Taichu; in the eleventh month, jiazi new moon at dawn winter's midpoint, heaven's calendar began to change; it was established in the Bright Hall, and the host of spirits received their registers.
translations.idiomatic: Five years on came Taichu 1: the winter solstice dawned on a jiazi new moon in the eleventh month, the calendar was reformed, orders went out from the Bright Hall, and the gods received their rosters.

sentences.zh: 太史公曰：「先人有言「『自周公卒五百歲而有孔子，孔子至於今五百歲，有能紹而明之，正《易傳》，繼《春秋》，本《詩》、《書》、《禮》、《樂》之際。』

translations.literal: The grand scribe said, "The ancestors had a saying: 'From the Duke of Zhou's death five hundred years and then there was Confucius; from Confucius to now five hundred years—should there be one able to continue and clarify it, rectify the Commentary on the Changes, succeed the Spring and Autumn, and root it in the juncture of the Odes, Documents, Rites, and Music.'"
translations.idiomatic: The grand scribe said: "Our forebears held that five centuries after the Duke of Zhou came Confucius, and five centuries after Confucius someone must carry the torch—rectifying the Changes commentary, continuing the Spring and Autumn, grounding it in the Odes, Documents, Rites, and Music."

sentences.zh: 意在斯乎！

translations.literal: The meaning lies herein!
translations.idiomatic: The mandate lies here!

sentences.zh: 意在斯乎！

translations.literal: The meaning lies herein!
translations.idiomatic: Is not the burden here?

sentences.zh: 小子何敢攘焉！」

translations.literal: How should a young man dare usurp that!"
translations.idiomatic: How dare a junior like me claim that task!"

sentences.zh: 上大夫壺遂曰：「昔孔子為何作《春秋》哉？」

translations.literal: Senior grandee Hu Sui said, "In old days why did Confucius compose the Spring and Autumn?"
translations.idiomatic: Hu Sui asked: "Why did Confucius write the Spring and Autumn?"

sentences.zh: 太史公曰：「余聞之董生：『周道廢，孔子為魯司寇，諸侯害之，大夫壅之。

translations.literal: The grand scribe said, "I heard it from Master Dong: 'Zhou's way was cast aside; Confucius became Lu's minister of crime; the feudal lords harmed him, great officers blocked him.
translations.idiomatic: The grand scribe answered: "Dong Zhongshu told me: 'When the Zhou way collapsed, Confucius took office in Lu; the lords feared him, ministers hemmed him in.'"

sentences.zh: 孔子知時之不用，道之不行也，是非二百四十二年之中，以為天下儀表，貶諸侯，討大夫，以達王事而已矣。』

translations.literal: Confucius knew the times would not employ him, the way would not be practiced; he judged right and wrong within two hundred forty-two years, took it as the model for all under heaven, demoted feudal lords, censured great officers, only to bring kingly affairs to light.'"
translations.idiomatic: Knowing his age would not use him, he read two hundred forty-two years of history as a mirror for the realm—cutting down arrogant lords and ministers to reveal the kingly thread.'"

sentences.zh: 子曰：『我欲載之空言，不如見之於行事之深切著明也。』

translations.literal: The Master said, 'I wished to set it forth in empty words, but it is not as good as seeing it in deeds, deep, cutting, and clear.'"
translations.idiomatic: As the Master put it, he would rather show the point in deeds than preach it in hollow words."

sentences.zh: 《春秋》上明三王之道，下辨人事之經紀，別嫌疑，明是非，定猶與，善善惡惡，賢賢賤不肖，存亡國，繼絕世，補弊起廢，王道之大者也。

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn above clarifies the way of the three kings, below distinguishes the warp and woof of human affairs, separates what is doubtful, clarifies right and wrong, settles hesitation, esteems the good and hates the bad, honors the worthy and demeans the unworthy, preserves perishing states, continues cut-off lines, mends decay and raises the fallen—the greatness of the kingly way lies herein.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn lights the way of the ancient kings above and sorts human affairs below—sorting doubt from certainty, right from wrong, praising good and condemning evil, exalting worth and shaming baseness, saving doomed states and broken lines, healing what was broken: that is the kingly way in full.

sentences.zh: 《易》，著天地、陰陽、四時、五行，故長於變；

translations.literal: The Changes manifests heaven and earth, Yin and Yang, four seasons, and five phases; therefore it excels at change;
translations.idiomatic: The Changes maps heaven, earth, yin and yang, seasons, and phases—so it masters transformation;

sentences.zh: 《禮》，綱紀人倫，故長於行；

translations.literal: The Rites cords and net human relations; therefore it excels at conduct;
translations.idiomatic: The Rites bind human relations—so it governs conduct;

sentences.zh: 《書》，記先王之事，故長於政；

translations.literal: The Documents records former kings' affairs; therefore it excels at government;
translations.idiomatic: The Documents records the deeds of past kings—so it serves statecraft;

sentences.zh: 《詩》，記山川、溪谷、禽獸、草木、牝牡、雌雄，故長於風；

translations.literal: The Odes records mountains and rivers, streams and valleys, birds and beasts, plants and trees, female and male; therefore it excels in the airs;
translations.idiomatic: The Odes paints landscape and creature, male and female—so it catches the folk airs;

sentences.zh: 《樂》，樂所以立，故長於和；

translations.literal: The Music is what music relies on to stand; therefore it excels at harmony;
translations.idiomatic: The Music shows what harmony rests on—so it cultivates concord;

sentences.zh: 《春秋》，辯是非，故長於治人。

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn distinguishes right and wrong; therefore it excels at governing men.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn judges right and wrong—so it teaches how to rule men.

sentences.zh: 是故《禮》以節人，《樂》以發和，《書》以道事，《詩》以達意，《易》以道化，《春秋》以道義。

translations.literal: Therefore the Rites by them restrain men, the Music by them issues harmony, the Documents by them convey affairs, the Odes by them express intent, the Changes by them convey transformation, the Spring and Autumn by them convey duty.
translations.idiomatic: Thus the Rites curb conduct, the Music releases harmony, the Documents narrate policy, the Odes voice feeling, the Changes speak of change, and the Spring and Autumn speaks of moral duty.

sentences.zh: 撥亂世反之正，莫近於《春秋》。

translations.literal: To set aright a chaotic age and return it to correctness, nothing comes nearer than the Spring and Autumn.
translations.idiomatic: Nothing brings a crooked age back to the straight like the Spring and Autumn.

sentences.zh: 《春秋》文成數萬，其指數千。

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn's text forms several myriads of graphs, its import several thousand.
translations.idiomatic: The text runs to tens of thousands of characters, its meanings to thousands.

sentences.zh: 萬物之散聚皆在《春秋》。

translations.literal: The myriad things' scattering and gathering are all in the Spring and Autumn.
translations.idiomatic: Every rise and fall of things lies folded in the Spring and Autumn.

sentences.zh: 《春秋》之中，弒君三十六，亡國五十二，諸侯奔走不得保社稷者不可勝數。

translations.literal: Within the Spring and Autumn, rulers murdered were thirty-six, states lost fifty-two, feudal lords who fled in disorder and could not preserve altars of soil and grain were beyond counting.
translations.idiomatic: It records thirty-six regicides, fifty-two fallen states, and countless lords who fled and lost their altars.

sentences.zh: 察其所以，皆失其本已。

translations.literal: Examining the reasons for this, all had lost their root.
translations.idiomatic: Trace each disaster and you find the root neglected.

sentences.zh: 故《易》曰『差以豪氂，謬以千里』。

translations.literal: Therefore the Changes says, 'Err by a hair's breadth, miss by a thousand li.'
translations.idiomatic: Hence the Changes: "A hair's-width error opens a thousand li of wrong."

sentences.zh: 故『臣弒君，子弒父，非一朝一夕之故，其漸久矣』。

translations.literal: Therefore 'ministers murdering lords, sons murdering fathers—these are not the work of one morning or evening; their gradual growth has been long.'
translations.idiomatic: Hence "regicide and parricide do not spring up overnight—they creep in over long years."

sentences.zh: 有國者不可以不知《春秋》，前有讒而不見，後有賊而不知。

translations.literal: Those who possess a state cannot fail to know the Spring and Autumn; ahead they have slanderers yet do not see them, behind they have traitors yet do not know them.
translations.idiomatic: No ruler can afford to ignore the Spring and Autumn: without it he misses slander in front and treachery behind.

sentences.zh: 為人臣者不可以不知《春秋》，守經事而不知其宜，遭變事而不知其權。

translations.literal: Those who are ministers cannot fail to know the Spring and Autumn; guarding routine affairs yet not knowing what is fitting, meeting changed affairs yet not knowing what is expedient.
translations.idiomatic: No minister can ignore it: in calm times he will misjudge what is right, in crisis he will miss the proper expedient.

sentences.zh: 為人君父者而不通於《春秋》之義者，必蒙首惡之名。

translations.literal: Those who are lords and fathers yet do not penetrate the Spring and Autumn's meanings will surely bear the name of chief evildoer.
translations.idiomatic: A father-ruler who does not grasp its lessons wears the brand of arch-villain.

sentences.zh: 為人臣子不通於《春秋》之義者，必陷篡弒誅死之罪。

translations.literal: Those who are ministers and sons yet do not penetrate the Spring and Autumn's meanings will surely fall into the crimes of usurpation, regicide, execution, and death.
translations.idiomatic: Sons and subjects who miss its point invite charges of usurpation, murder, and capital crime.

sentences.zh: 其實皆為善為之，而不知其義，被之空言不敢辭。

translations.literal: In fact they all acted intending good, yet did not know its meaning; bearing empty reproach they dare not decline.
translations.idiomatic: They often meant well but missed the principle, then could not escape the verdict of history.

sentences.zh: 夫不通禮義之指，至於君不君，臣不臣，父不父，子不子。

translations.literal: As for not penetrating the point of ritual and duty, it reaches the point where the lord is no lord, the minister no minister, the father no father, the son no son.
translations.idiomatic: Without ritual and duty, lords cease to rule, ministers to serve, fathers to guide, sons to obey.

sentences.zh: 夫君不君則犯，臣不臣則誅，父不父則無道，子不子則不孝：此四行者，天下之大過也。

translations.literal: As for the lord not being lord, then he is violated; the minister not being minister, then he is executed; the father not being father, then there is no way; the son not being son, then there is no filial piety: these four conducts are the great faults of all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: A false lord is attacked, a false minister killed, a false father is lawless, a false son unfilial—these are the worst crimes under heaven.

sentences.zh: 以天下大過予之，受而不敢辭。

translations.literal: To apply to them the great faults of all under heaven—they receive them and dare not decline.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn pins those great faults on them, and they cannot shrug the name off.

sentences.zh: 故《春秋》者，禮義之大宗也。

translations.literal: Therefore the Spring and Autumn is the great lineage of ritual and duty.
translations.idiomatic: Thus the Spring and Autumn is the great charter of ritual and right.

sentences.zh: 夫禮禁未然之前，法施已然之後；

translations.literal: As for ritual, it forbids before it has happened; as for law, it applies after it has already happened;
translations.idiomatic: Ritual checks evil before the fact; law punishes after the fact;

sentences.zh: 法之所為用者易見，而禮之所為禁者難知。」

translations.literal: what law does in being applied is easy to see, while what ritual does in forbidding is hard to know."
translations.idiomatic: law's work is plain to the eye, ritual's prevention is subtle."

sentences.zh: 壺遂曰：「孔子之時，上無明君，下不得任用，故作《春秋》，垂空文以斷禮義，當一王之法。

translations.literal: Hu Sui said, "In Confucius's time, above there was no enlightened lord, below he could not obtain employment; therefore he composed the Spring and Autumn, hung empty text to judge ritual and duty, and matched the laws of one king.
translations.idiomatic: Hu Sui said: "Confucius had no worthy ruler above and no office below, so he wrote the Spring and Autumn—pure text that would stand as kingly law for ritual and right."

sentences.zh: 今夫子上遇明天子，下得守職，萬事既具，咸各序其宜，夫子所論，欲以何明？」

translations.literal: Now as for you, sir, above you meet an enlightened Son of Heaven, below you obtain to keep your post; the myriad affairs are already complete, all each ordered in their fitness—what is it your discourse wishes to clarify?"
translations.idiomatic: You serve a brilliant sovereign and hold a steady post; every office runs as it should—what, then, do you mean to illuminate?"

sentences.zh: 太史公曰：「唯唯，否否，不然。

translations.literal: The grand scribe said, "Yes yes, no no—it is not so.
translations.idiomatic: The grand scribe said: "Yes and no—not quite."

sentences.zh: 余聞之先人曰：『虙戲至純厚，作《易》八卦。

translations.literal: I heard it from my forbears: 'Fuxi was utmost pure and thick; he made the Changes' eight trigrams.
translations.idiomatic: My father used to say that Fuxi, in utter simplicity, first drew the eight trigrams of the Changes.

sentences.zh: 堯、舜之盛，《尚書》載之，禮樂作焉。

translations.literal: Yao and Shun's splendor, the Documents records it; rites and music were made therefrom.
translations.idiomatic: The glory of Yao and Shun fills the Documents, and ritual and music grew from that age.

sentences.zh: 湯、武之降，詩人歌之。

translations.literal: Tang and Wu's decline, the poets sang it.
translations.idiomatic: The poets sang the rise and turn of Tang and Wu.

sentences.zh: 《春秋》采善貶惡，推三代之德，褒周室，非獨刺譏而已也。』

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn gathers good and demotes evil, extends the virtue of the three ages, praises the Zhou house—it is not only censure and ridicule.'
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn praises good, blames evil, carries the three dynasties' virtue forward, and honors Zhou—it is more than satire alone.

sentences.zh: 漢興已來，至明天子，獲符瑞，封禪，改正朔，易服色，受命於穆清，澤流罔極，海外殊俗，重譯款塞，請來獻見者，不可勝道。

translations.literal: From Han's rise to the enlightened Son of Heaven, obtaining tallies and omens, feng and shan, changing the first month of the year, altering dress colors, receiving the mandate in vast purity, bounty flowing without limit, beyond the seas strange customs, double-interpreted coming to the passes, requesting to come present audience—cannot be fully told.
translations.idiomatic: Since Han rose down to our enlightened sovereign—omens gathered, feng and shan performed, calendar and vestments reformed, the mandate received in silent heaven, grace flooding without end, foreign peoples thronging the frontier with tribute beyond counting—

sentences.zh: 臣下百官，力誦聖德，猶不能宣盡其意。

translations.literal: Ministers below and the hundred offices, striving to chant the sage virtue, still cannot declare and exhaust its meaning.
translations.idiomatic: the hundred officials strain to hymn his holiness yet cannot exhaust it.

sentences.zh: 且士賢能矣，而不用，有國者恥也；

translations.literal: Moreover when a knight is worthy and able yet is not employed, it is the shame of him who possesses a state;
translations.idiomatic: To leave a worthy man unused shames the throne;

sentences.zh: 主上明聖，德不布聞，有司之過也。

translations.literal: when the lord above is enlightened and sage yet his virtue is not spread and heard, it is the fault of those in charge.
translations.idiomatic: if a sage ruler's virtue goes unreported, the officials have failed.

sentences.zh: 且余掌其官，廢明聖盛德不載，滅功臣、賢大夫之業不述，墮先人所言，罪莫大焉。

translations.literal: Moreover I grasp its office; to cast aside enlightened sage splendor and not record it, to extinguish merit ministers' and worthy great officers' undertakings and not narrate them, to ruin what forbears spoke of—no crime is greater.
translations.idiomatic: I hold this office: if I let sage brilliance go unwritten, let the deeds of heroes and good ministers fade, I betray my father's charge—there is no greater crime.

sentences.zh: 余所謂述故事，整齊其世傳，非所謂作也，而君比之《春秋》，謬矣。」

translations.literal: What I call narrating old affairs, ordering their transmitted generations, is not what is called making; yet you, sir, compare it to the Spring and Autumn—wrong."
translations.idiomatic: I only arrange old stories and set chronicles in order—I am not "making" a classic as Confucius did; to liken my work to the Spring and Autumn is mistaken."

sentences.zh: 於是論次其文。

translations.literal: Thereupon he discussed and ordered his text.
translations.idiomatic: He then set his manuscript in order.

sentences.zh: 十年而遭李陵之禍，幽於累紲。

translations.literal: In the tenth year he met with Li Ling's disaster and was imprisoned in fetters.
translations.idiomatic: Ten years in came the Li Ling affair and prison chains.

sentences.zh: 乃喟然而歎曰：「是余之罪夫！

translations.literal: He then sighed deeply and said, "This is my crime, is it not!
translations.idiomatic: He sighed, "My own fault!

sentences.zh: 身虧不用矣。」

translations.literal: The body is marred—there is no more employment."
translations.idiomatic: My body is ruined—useless now."

sentences.zh: 退而深惟曰：「夫《詩》、《書》隱約者，欲遂其志之思也。」

translations.literal: He withdrew and thought deeply, saying, "As for the Odes and Documents being cryptic, it is the thought of wishing to fulfill one's intent."
translations.idiomatic: Brooding alone he said, "The Odes and Documents speak in veiled words because their authors meant to voice what they could not say outright."

sentences.zh: 卒述陶唐以來，至於麟止，自黃帝始。

translations.literal: In the end he narrated from Pottery Tang down to stopping at the unicorn, beginning from the Yellow Thearch.
translations.idiomatic: He carried the narrative from the Yellow Thearch through Yao's line down to the capture of the unicorn.

sentences.zh: 《五帝本紀》第一，《夏本紀》第二，《殷本紀》第三，《周本紀》第四，《秦本紀》第五，《始皇本紀》第六，《項羽本紀》第七，《高祖本紀》第八，《呂後本紀》第九，《孝文本紀》第十，《孝景本紀》第十一，《今上本紀》第十二。

translations.literal: "Basic Annals of the Five Thearchs" first; "Basic Annals of Xia" second; "Basic Annals of Yin" third; "Basic Annals of Zhou" fourth; "Basic Annals of Qin" fifth; "Basic Annals of the First Emperor" sixth; "Basic Annals of Xiang Yu" seventh; "Basic Annals of Gaozu" eighth; "Basic Annals of Empress Lü" ninth; "Basic Annals of Emperor Xiaowen" tenth; "Basic Annals of Emperor Xiaojing" eleventh; "Basic Annals of the Reigning Sovereign" twelfth.
translations.idiomatic: Twelve basic annals: from the Five Thearchs through Xia, Yin, Zhou, Qin, the First Emperor, Xiang Yu, Gaozu, Empress Lü, Emperors Wen and Jing, to the reigning emperor.

sentences.zh: 《三代世表》第一，《十二諸侯年表》第二，《六國年表》第三，《秦楚之際月表》第四，《漢諸侯年表》第五，《高祖功臣年表》第六，《惠景間功臣年表》第七，《建元以來侯者年表》第八，《王子侯者年表》第九，《漢興以來將相名臣年表》第十。

translations.literal: "Genealogical Table of the Three Ages" first; "Yearly Table of the Twelve Feudal Lords" second; "Yearly Table of the Six States" third; "Monthly Table of the Qin-Chu Interregnum" fourth; "Yearly Table of Han Feudal Lords" fifth; "Yearly Table of Gaozu's Merit Ministers" sixth; "Yearly Table of Merit Ministers between Hui and Jing" seventh; "Yearly Table of Marquises since Jianyuan" eighth; "Yearly Table of Princely Marquises" ninth; "Yearly Table of Generals, Ministers, and Noted Officials since Han's Rise" tenth.
translations.idiomatic: Ten tables: royal genealogy, the twelve lords, the six states, the Qin-Chu interlude month by month, Han feudatories, Gaozu's ministers, the Hui-Jing ministers, marquises since Jianyuan, princely marquises, and Han's generals and ministers.

sentences.zh: 《禮書》第一，《樂書》第二，《律書》第三，《歷書》第四，《天官書》第五，《封禪書》第六，《河渠書》第七，《平准書》第八。

translations.literal: "Treatise on Rites" first; "Treatise on Music" second; "Treatise on Pitch-pipes" third; "Treatise on the Calendar" fourth; "Treatise on Celestial Offices" fifth; "Treatise on the Feng and Shan Sacrifices" sixth; "Treatise on Rivers and Canals" seventh; "Treatise on the Balanced Standard" eighth.
translations.idiomatic: Eight treatises: rites, music, pitch and law, calendrics, astronomy, the feng and shan rites, hydraulics, and state finance.

sentences.zh: 《吳太伯世家》第一，《齊太公世家》第二，《魯周公世家》第三，《燕召公世家》第四，《管蔡世家》第五，《陳杞世家》第六，《衛康叔世家》第七，《宋微子世家》第八，《晉世家》第九，《楚世家》第十，《越世家》第十一，《鄭世家》第十二，《趙世家》第十三，《魏世家》第十四，《韓世家》第十五，《田完世家》第十六，《孔子世家》第十七，《陳涉世家》第十八，《外戚世家》第十九，《楚元王世家》第二十，《荊燕王世家》第二十一，《齊悼惠王世家》第二十二，《蕭相國世家》第二十三，《曹相國世家》第二十四，《留侯世家》第二十五，《陳丞相世家》第二十六，《絳侯世家》第二十七，《梁孝王世家》第二十八，《五宗世家》第二十九，《三王世家》第三十。

translations.literal: He lists the thirty hereditary houses in order from Wu Taibo through the Three Kings, covering the great Zhou feudatories, Warring States houses, Confucius, Chen She, Han imperial affines and princes, and founding ministers such as Xiao He and Cao Shen.
translations.idiomatic: The thirty hereditary houses open with Wu Taibo and the eastern houses of Qi, Lu, Yan, Guan and Cai, Chen and Qi, Wei, Song, Jin, Chu, Yue, and Zheng, continue through the Warring States lines of Zhao, Wei, Han, and Tian Wan, add Confucius and Chen She, then turn to Han with the affinal houses, the kings of Chu, Jing, and Yan, Prince Dao Hui of Qi, founding ministers Xiao He and Cao Shen, strategists Zhang Liang and Chen Ping, the line of Marquis Jiang, Prince Xiao of Liang, the Five Clans, and the Three Kings—each scroll a lineage of power from Zhou feudalism into the Han empire.

sentences.zh: 《伯夷列傳》經一，《管晏列傳》第二，《老子韓非列傳》第三，《司與穰苴列傳》第四，《孫子吳起列傳》第五，《伍子胥列傳》第六，《仲尼弟子列傳》第七，《商君列傳》第八，《蘇秦列傳》第九，《張儀列傳》第十，《樗裡甘茂列傳》第十一，《穰侯列傳》第十二，《白起王翦列傳》第十三，《孟子荀卿列傳》第十四，《平原虞卿列傳》第十五，《孟嘗君列傳》第十六，《魏公子列傳》第十七，《春申君列傳》第十八，《范睢蔡澤列傳》第十九，《樂毅列傳》第二十，《廉頗藺相如列傳》第二十一，《田單列傳》第二十二，《魯仲連列傳》第二十三，《屈原賈生列傳》第二十四，《呂不韋列傳》第二十五，《刺客列傳》第二十六，《李斯列傳》第二十七，《蒙恬列傳》第二十八，《張耳陳餘列傳》第二十九，《魏豹彭越列傳》第三十，《黥布列傳》第三十一，《淮陰侯韓信列傳》第三十二，《韓王信盧綰列傳》第三十三，《田儋列傳》第三十四，《樊酈滕灌列傳》第三十五，《張丞相倉列傳》第三十六，《酈生陸賈列傳》第三十七，《傅靳崩阜成侯列傳》第三十八，《劉敬叔孫通列傳》第三十九，《季布欒布列傳》第四十，《爰盎朝錯列傳》第四十一，《張釋之馮唐列傳》第四十二，《萬石張叔列傳》第四十三，《田叔列傳》第四十四，《扁鵲倉公列傳》第四十五，《吳王濞列傳》第四十六，《魏其武安列傳》第四十七，《韓長孺列傳》第四十八，《李將軍列傳》第四十九，《衛將軍驃騎列傳》第五十，《平津主父列傳》第五十一，《匈奴列傳》第五十二，《南越列傳》第五十三，《閩越列傳》第五十四，《朝鮮列傳》第五十五，《西南夷列傳》第五十六，《司馬相如列傳》第五十七，《淮南衡山列傳》第五十八，《循吏列傳》第五十九，《汲鄭列傳》第六十，《儒林列傳》第六十一，《酷吏列傳》第六十二，《大宛列傳》第六十三，《遊俠列傳》第六十四，《佞幸列傳》第六十五，《滑稽列傳》第六十六，《日者列傳》第六十七，《龜策列傳》第六十八，《貨殖列傳》第六十九。

translations.literal: "Biographies of Bo Yi" first (text reads 經 for 第一), "Biographies of Guan Zhong and Yan Ying" second, "Biographies of Laozi and Han Fei" third, "Biographies of Sima Rangju" fourth (text reads 司與 for 司馬), "Biographies of Sunzi and Wu Qi" fifth, "Biographies of Wu Zixu" sixth, "Biographies of Confucius's Disciples" seventh, "Biographies of Lord Shang" eighth, "Biographies of Su Qin" ninth, "Biographies of Zhang Yi" tenth, "Biographies of Chulizi and Gan Mao" eleventh, "Biographies of the Marquis of Rang" twelfth, "Biographies of Bai Qi and Wang Jian" thirteenth, "Biographies of Mencius and Xun Qing" fourteenth, "Biographies of Lord Pingyuan and Yu Qing" fifteenth, "Biographies of Lord Mengchang" sixteenth, "Biographies of Lord Xinling" seventeenth, "Biographies of Lord "Biographies of Lu Zhonglian" twenty-third, "Biographies of Qu Yuan and Jia Yi" twenty-fourth, "Biographies of Lü Buwei" twenty-fifth, "Biographies of Assassins" twenty-sixth, "Biographies of Li Si" twenty-seventh, "Biographies of Meng Tian" twenty-eighth, "Biographies of Zhang Er and Chen Yu" twenty-ninth, "Biographies of Wei Bao and Peng Yue" thirtieth, "Biographies of Qing Bu" thirty-first, and Guan Ying" thirty-fifth, "Biographies of Chancellor Zhang Cang" thirty-sixth, "Biographies of Li Yiji and Lu Jia" thirty-seventh, "Biographies of Fu Kuan and Marquis of Fucheng" thirty-eighth,
