translations.idiomatic: The sixty-nine biographies (the manuscript garbles two titles) run from Bo Yi and Shu Qi through Guan Zhong and Yan Ying, Laozi and Han Fei, Sima Rangju, Sun Wu and Wu Qi, Wu Zixu, Confucius's disciples, Lord Shang, Su Qin and Zhang Yi, Chulizi and Gan Mao, the Marquis of Rang, Bai Qi and Wang Jian, Mencius and Xun Qing, the lords Pingyuan, Mengchang, Xinling, and Chunshen, Fan Ju and Cai Ze, Yue Yi, Lian Po and Lin Xiangru, Tian Dan, Lu Zhonglian, Qu Yuan and Jia Yi, Lü Buwei, the assassins, Li Si and Meng Tian, Zhang Er and Chen Yu, Wei Bao and Peng Yue, Qing Bu, Han Xin and his peers, Tian Dan of Qi, Fan Kuai's circle, Zhang Cang, Li Yiji and Lu Jia, Fu Kuan, Liu Jing and Shu Sun Tong, Ji Bu, Yuan Ang, Zhang Shizhi, Wan Shi, Tian Shu, the physicians, Prince Liu Pi, Dou Ying and Tian Fen, Han Anguo, Li Guang, Wei Qing and Huo Qubing, Gongsun Hong, the frontier chapters on Xiongnu, Nanyue, Minyue, Korea, and the southwestern tribes, Sima Xiangru, Huainan, good and harsh officials, Confucian scholars, Dayuan, wandering knights, favorites, jesters, diviners, turtle divination, and merchants—an atlas of character across ages.

sentences.zh: 惟漢繼五帝末流，接三代絕業。

translations.literal: Only Han continues the Five Thearchs' late current, links to the three ages' cut-off enterprise.
translations.idiomatic: Han alone carries the tail of the Five Thearchs and picks up the broken thread of the three dynasties.

sentences.zh: 周道既廢，秦撥去古文，焚滅《詩》、《書》，故明堂、石室、金鐀、玉版圖籍散亂。

translations.literal: Zhou's way was already cast aside; Qin cast away ancient script, burned and destroyed the Odes and Documents; therefore Bright Hall, stone chambers, metal chests, and jade-tablet charts and records were scattered in disorder.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou's order was gone; Qin purged old texts and burned the classics, so palace archives in stone vaults and metal coffers fell into chaos.

sentences.zh: 漢興，蕭何次律令，韓信申軍法，張蒼為章程，叔孫通定禮儀，則文學彬彬稍進，《詩》、《書》往往間出。

translations.literal: When Han rose, Xiao He ordered statutes and ordinances, Han Xin extended military law, Zhang Cang made regulations and models, Shu Sun Tong fixed ritual and ceremony; then letters and learning gradually advanced, the Odes and Documents often emerged in patches.
translations.idiomatic: Under Han, Xiao He codified law, Han Xin the military codes, Zhang Cang the bureaucratic rules, Shu Sun Tong court ritual—learning revived and fragments of the Odes and Documents resurfaced.

sentences.zh: 自曹參薦蓋公言黃、老，而賈誼、韓錯明申、朝，公孫弘以儒顯，百年之間，天下遺文古事靡不畢集。

translations.literal: From Cao Shen recommending Master Gai who spoke of Huang and Lao, while Jia Yi and Han Cuo (i.e. Chao Cuo) clarified Shen and Shang, Gongsun Hong by Ru became eminent—within a hundred years, all under heaven's stray writings and old affairs none were not fully gathered.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Shen's patronage of Huang-Lao, Jia Yi and Chao Cuo's Legalism, Gongsun Hong's Confucian rise—within a century every scrap of old text and story was being gathered.

sentences.zh: 太史公仍父子相繼籑其職，曰：「於戲！

translations.literal: The grand scribe continuing father and son in succession compiling this office, said, "Alas!
translations.idiomatic: The grand scribe, father and son in this same office, declared: "Alas!

sentences.zh: 余維先人嘗掌斯事，顯於唐、虞；

translations.literal: I consider how my forbears once grasped this affair, were eminent in Tang and Yu;
translations.idiomatic: My ancestors held this charge under Yao and Shun;

sentences.zh: 至於周，復典之。

translations.literal: down to Zhou, again they presided over it.
translations.idiomatic: in Zhou they held it again.

sentences.zh: 故司馬氏世主天宮，至於余乎，欽念哉！」

translations.literal: Therefore the Sima clan for generations presided over heaven's palace—down to me! Reverently heed this!"
translations.idiomatic: So the Simas have watched heaven's offices for generations—down to me. Remember!"

sentences.zh: 網羅天下放失舊聞，王跡所興，原始察終，見盛觀衰，論考之行事，略三代，錄秦、漢，上記軒轅，下至於茲，著十二本紀；

translations.literal: He netted all under heaven's scattered and lost old hearsay, where kingly traces arose, sought beginnings and examined ends, saw flourishing and observed decline, discussed and examined them in deeds and conduct, summarized the three ages, recorded Qin and Han, upward recorded Xuanyuan, downward reached to here, and wrote twelve basic annals;
translations.idiomatic: He gathered lost lore, traced where kingship rose and fell from the Yellow Thearch through three ages, Qin, and Han to the present, and set it down in twelve basic annals;

sentences.zh: 既科條之矣，並時異世，年差不明，作十表；

translations.literal: having already itemized them, concurrent times and different ages, years and differences unclear, he made ten tables;
translations.idiomatic: then, because parallel ages were hard to align by year alone, he added ten tables;

sentences.zh: 禮樂損益，律歷改易，兵權、山川、鬼神，天人之際，承敝通變，作八書；

translations.literal: rites and music's increase and decrease, pitch-pipes and calendar's changes, military authority, mountains and rivers, ghosts and spirits, heaven and man's juncture, inheriting decay and penetrating change—he made eight treatises;
translations.idiomatic: for ritual, music, law, calendar, war, rivers, spirits, and heaven's relation to man—eight treatises on change through decay and renewal;

sentences.zh: 二十八宿環北辰，三十輻共一轂，運行無窮，輔弼股肱之臣配焉，忠信行道以奉主上，作三十世家；

translations.literal: the twenty-eight lodges ring the north pole; thirty spokes share one hub; turning and moving without end—supporting thigh and arm ministers match them, loyally and faithfully practicing the way to serve the lord above—he made thirty hereditary houses;
translations.idiomatic: like spokes round the hub he paired loyal ministers who bore the throne on their backs—thirty hereditary houses;

sentences.zh: 扶義俶儻，不令己失時，立功名於天下，作七十列傳：凡百三十篇，五十二萬六千五百字，為《太史公書》。

translations.literal: upholding righteousness and standing apart, not letting themselves miss the moment, establishing merit and fame within all under heaven—he made seventy ranked biographies: in all one hundred thirty sections, five hundred twenty-six thousand five hundred graphs, making the Grand Scribe's Documents.
translations.idiomatic: and for men who seized the hour and won fame in the world—seventy biographies: one hundred thirty chapters, 526,500 characters, the book called the Grand Scribe's Documents.

sentences.zh: 序略，以拾遺補闕，成一家言，協《六經》異傳，齊百家雜語，臧之名山，副在京師，以俟後聖君子。

translations.literal: Summaries and outlines, to pick up omissions and mend gaps, complete one family's words, harmonize the Six Classics' divergent transmissions, align the hundred schools' mixed sayings, store them on a famous mountain, duplicate in the capital, to await later sage gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Prefaces fill gaps, forge a single voice, reconcile variant traditions of the six classics and hundred schools, lodge a copy on a sacred peak and another in the capital, and wait for a later sage.

sentences.zh: 第七十，遷之自敘云爾。

translations.literal: The seventieth—Qian's self-narration ends thus.
translations.idiomatic: The seventieth scroll is Qian's own epilogue.

sentences.zh: 而十篇缺，有錄無書。

translations.literal: Yet ten sections are missing—there are registers but no books.
translations.idiomatic: Ten chapters survive only as titles—the text was lost.

sentences.zh: 遷既被刑之後，為中書令，尊寵任職。

translations.literal: After Qian had suffered mutilation, he became palace secretary, honored and favored in office.
translations.idiomatic: After castration Qian became palace secretary, favored at court.

sentences.zh: 故人益州刺史任安予遷書，責以古賢臣之義。

translations.literal: His old friend Regional Inspector of Yi Ren An sent Qian a letter, reproaching him by the duty of ancient worthy ministers.
translations.idiomatic: His old friend Ren An, governor of Yi province, wrote urging him to live up to the old standard of loyal ministers.

sentences.zh: 遷報之曰：

translations.literal: Qian replied to him, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Qian answered:

sentences.zh: 少卿足下：曩者辱賜書，教以慎於接物，推賢進士為務。

translations.literal: Sir Shaoqing: formerly you deigned to send a letter, teaching me to be careful in dealing with others and to make recommending the worthy and advancing knights my task.
translations.idiomatic: Sir Shaoqing: you did me honor with your letter, urging care in dealing with men and making the promotion of worth my duty.

sentences.zh: 意氣勤勤懇懇，若望僕不相師用，而流俗人之言。

translations.literal: Your intent was diligent and earnest, as if resenting that your servant would not take you as teacher and employ you, and instead followed vulgar men's words.
translations.idiomatic: You pressed your case as though I refused your counsel and heeded only the gossip of the crowd.

sentences.zh: 僕非敢如是也。

translations.literal: Your servant does not dare to be thus.
translations.idiomatic: I would not dare that.

sentences.zh: 雖罷駑，亦嘗側聞長者遺風矣。

translations.literal: Although I am a worn-out nag, I have also once overheard the surviving airs of elders.
translations.idiomatic: Though I am only a broken nag, I have caught whispers of how the old gentlemen behaved.

sentences.zh: 顧自以為身殘處穢，動而見尤，欲益反損，是以抑鬱而無誰語。

translations.literal: Only I consider myself mutilated in body and dwelling in filth; moving, I meet blame; wishing to help, I instead harm—therefore I am depressed and have no one to speak to.
translations.idiomatic: But I am a mutilated man in a foul station: every move draws blame, every try to help backfires, so I choke in silence with no one to hear me.

sentences.zh: 諺曰：「誰為為之，孰令聽之？」

translations.literal: The proverb says, "Who will do it for him—who will order it heard?"
translations.idiomatic: The proverb asks, "Who would speak for me—and who would listen?"

sentences.zh: 蓋鍾子期死，伯牙終身不復鼓琴。

translations.literal: When Zhong Ziqi died, Bo Ya all his life never again plucked the zither.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhong Ziqi died, Bo Ya never touched his strings again.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 士為知已用，女為說己容。

translations.literal: A knight acts for one who knows him; a woman adorns herself for one who delights in her.
translations.idiomatic: A man gives his strength to the friend who knows him; a woman paints herself for the eye that loves her.

sentences.zh: 若僕大質已虧缺，雖材懷隨、行，行若由、夷，終不可以為榮，適足以發笑而自點耳。

translations.literal: As for your servant, my great substance is already marred and lacking; though my talent held Sui and He (the pearl and jade), my conduct were like Xu You and Bo Yi—in the end it cannot be taken as glory, it is quite enough to draw laughter and smirch myself.
translations.idiomatic: My substance is ruined: even with gifts like the Sui pearl and He's jade in talent, and the purity of Xu You and Bo Yi in conduct, I could not wear them as honor—only as a joke and a stain.

sentences.zh: 書辭宜答，會東從上來，又迫賤事，相見日淺，卒卒無須臾之間得竭指意。

translations.literal: The letter's words ought to be answered, but it happened I followed the sovereign east and came back, again was pressed by mean tasks, the days we met were shallow, hastily without a moment's space to exhaust my intent.
translations.idiomatic: I owed you a reply, but I was trailing the emperor east, then swamped with petty duties; our meetings were brief and I never had a moment to lay out my mind.

sentences.zh: 今少卿抱不測之罪，涉旬月，迫季冬，僕又薄從上上雍，恐卒然不可諱。

translations.literal: Now Sir Shaoqing bears an unfathomable crime; it has crossed months; it presses the end of winter; your servant moreover is thinly following the sovereign up to Yong—I fear suddenly the unavoidable will come.
translations.idiomatic: Now you face a capital charge; months have passed and deep winter nears; I am to follow the sovereign to Yong—any day the worst may happen.

sentences.zh: 是僕終已不得舒憤懣以曉左右，則長逝者魂魄私恨無窮。

translations.literal: In this case your servant in the end cannot unburden resentment and vexation to make clear to those about you, then the long-gone one's ghost will harbor private regret without end.
translations.idiomatic: If I never unburden my heart to you, your spirit in death would nurse endless regret.

sentences.zh: 請略陳固陋。

translations.literal: I beg to set forth briefly my obstinate ignorance.
translations.idiomatic: Let me state my crude thoughts.

sentences.zh: 闕然不報，幸勿過。

translations.literal: Having been remiss and not answered, I beg you not to fault me.
translations.idiomatic: Forgive my long silence.

sentences.zh: 僕聞之：修身者，智之府也；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard: he who cultivates the person is wisdom's treasury;
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that self-cultivation is the storehouse of wisdom;

sentences.zh: 愛施者，仁之端也；

translations.literal: he who loves and gives is benevolence's beginning;
translations.idiomatic: that charity is the sprout of benevolence;

sentences.zh: 取予者，義之符也；

translations.literal: Taking and giving are duty's tally;
translations.idiomatic: Giving and taking mark where duty lies;

sentences.zh: 恥辱者，勇之決也；

translations.literal: shame and disgrace are courage's decision;
translations.idiomatic: shame and honor test a man's courage;

sentences.zh: 立名者，行之極也：士有此五者，然後可以托於世，列於君子之林矣。

translations.literal: establishing a name is conduct's utmost: a knight having these five may then be entrusted within the world and arrayed in the grove of gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: and making a name is the crown of conduct. With these five, a gentleman may stand in the world and walk among the virtuous.

sentences.zh: 故禍莫憯於欲利，悲莫痛於傷心，行莫醜於辱先，而詬莫大於官刑。

translations.literal: Therefore no calamity is more bitter than desiring profit, no grief more painful than wounding the heart, no conduct uglier than shaming ancestors, and no revilement greater than palace mutilation.
translations.idiomatic: No curse bites like greed for gain, no sorrow cuts like a broken heart, no disgrace stains like dishonoring forebears, and no insult cuts like the castrator's knife.

sentences.zh: 刑余之人，無所比數，非一也，所從來遠矣！

translations.literal: Mutilation-surviving men have no one to rank with—not one case; the source goes back far!
translations.idiomatic: Men who have known the knife are counted with no one—the shame is ancient.

sentences.zh: 昔衛靈公與雍渠載，孔子適陳；

translations.literal: Of old Duke Ling of Wei rode in the same carriage with Yong Qu—Confucius went off to Chen;
translations.idiomatic: Duke Ling of Wei shared his carriage with the eunuch Yong Qu, and Confucius left for Chen;

sentences.zh: 商鞅因景監見，趙良寒心；

translations.literal: Shang Yang gained audience through Jing Jian—Zhao Liang's heart turned cold;
translations.idiomatic: Shang Yang entered court through the eunuch Jing Jian, and Zhao Liang shuddered;

sentences.zh: 同子參乘，爰絲變色：自古而恥之。

translations.literal: Tongzi rode as outrider—Yuan Si changed color: from antiquity this has been shameful.
translations.idiomatic: when the eunuch Zhao Tong rode as guard, Yuan Ang blanched—such things have been shameful since antiquity.

sentences.zh: 夫中材之人，事關於宦豎，莫不傷氣，況慷慨之士乎！

translations.literal: As for middling-talent men, when affairs touch palace attendants, none fails to wound their spirit—how much more ardent knights!
translations.idiomatic: Even middling men lose heart when eunuchs are involved—what of a proud spirit?

sentences.zh: 如今朝雖乏人，奈何令刀鋸之餘薦天下豪雋哉！

translations.literal: Now though the court lacks men, how can you order the knife-and-saw's leavings to recommend the realm's heroes!
translations.idiomatic: The court may lack men, but how can the leavings of the executioner's block recommend heroes to the throne?

sentences.zh: 僕賴先人緒業，得待罪輦轂下，二十餘年矣。

translations.literal: Your servant relying on forbears' leftover enterprise, has been able to await punishment under the imperial chariot over twenty years.
translations.idiomatic: By my father's legacy I have served at the capital over twenty years.

sentences.zh: 所以自惟：上之，不能納忠效信，有奇策材力之譽，自結明主；

translations.literal: Therefore I reflect on myself: at the highest, I could not submit loyalty and show trustworthiness, have the fame of strange stratagems and material force, and bind myself to an enlightened lord;
translations.idiomatic: I have asked myself: I never won the ruler's ear with loyal counsel or a name for strategy and strength;

sentences.zh: 次之，又不能拾遺補闕，招賢進能，顯巖穴之士；

translations.literal: next, I could not pick up omissions and mend gaps, summon worthies and advance the able, display cave-dwelling scholars;
translations.idiomatic: nor mended policy gaps, raised hidden talent from reclusion;

sentences.zh: 外之，不能備行伍，攻城野戰，有斬將搴旗之功；

translations.literal: outwardly, I could not fill the ranks, storm cities or fight in the field, have merit of beheading generals and seizing banners;
translations.idiomatic: nor stood in the ranks to storm walls and win the glory of striking generals and snatching flags;

sentences.zh: 下之，不能累日積勞，取尊官厚祿，以為宗族交遊光寵。

translations.literal: at the lowest, I could not pile days and accumulate toil, obtain honored office and thick salary, thereby giving light and favor to clan and friends.
translations.idiomatic: nor toiled year on year for high rank and fat pay to bring honor to kin and friends.

sentences.zh: 四者無一遂，苟合取容，無所短長之效，可見於此矣。

translations.literal: Of the four not one succeeded; I barely conformed to win acceptance—no effect of either short or long—it is visible in this.
translations.idiomatic: I failed on every count, clung to office by mere conformity, and proved neither use nor harm—here is the record.

sentences.zh: 鄉者，僕亦嘗廁下大夫之列，陪外廷末議。

translations.literal: Formerly your servant also once squatted in the lower grandee ranks, attended outer-court tail discussions.
translations.idiomatic: Once I stood among the lower grandees at the outer court.

sentences.zh: 不以此時引維綱，盡思慮，今已虧形為掃除之隸，在闒茸之中，乃欲卬首信眉，論列是非，不亦輕朝廷，羞當世之士邪！

translations.literal: Not at that time drawing the great cords and nets, exhausting thought and care—now already mutilated in form as a sweeping slave's underling, among the vile and low—yet wishing to lift the head and stretch the brows, array right and wrong—is this not belittling the court and shaming the age's gentlemen!
translations.idiomatic: I did not then speak the truth with all my mind; now, maimed and fit only to sweep, I would raise my head and argue right and wrong—would that not insult the court and shame every gentleman of our day?

sentences.zh: 嗟乎！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 嗟乎！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas again!

sentences.zh: 如僕，尚何言哉！

translations.literal: As for your servant, what more is there to say!
translations.idiomatic: For one like me, what is left to say?

sentences.zh: 尚何言哉！

translations.literal: What more is there to say!
translations.idiomatic: What more can I say?

sentences.zh: 且事本末未易明也。

translations.literal: Moreover the affair's root and tip are not easy to make clear.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, the whole story is hard to explain to the crowd.

sentences.zh: 僕少負不羈之才，長無鄉曲之譽，主上幸以先人之故，使得奉薄技，出入周衛之中。

translations.literal: Your servant in youth bore unrestrained talent; grown, had no local praise; the sovereign fortunately by forbears' cause allowed me to present meager skill, going in and out within the surrounding guards.
translations.idiomatic: I was proud and unbridled in youth, unknown in my home town; the emperor, for my father's sake, let me offer my poor talents within the palace guard.

sentences.zh: 僕以為戴盆何以望天，故絕賓客之知，忘室家之業，日夜思竭其不肖之材力，務壹心營職，以求親媚於主上。

translations.literal: Your servant thought, with a basin on one's head how can one gaze at heaven; therefore I cut off guests' acquaintance, forgot household tasks, day and night thought to exhaust my unworthy material force, strove with one heart to manage duty, seeking thereby to draw close and please the sovereign.
translations.idiomatic: I thought, "You cannot look at the sky with a tub on your head," so I dropped friends and family, night and day poured my poor strength into one office, hoping to win the ruler's favor.

sentences.zh: 而事乃有大謬不然者。

translations.literal: Yet the affair then had a great error—not so.
translations.idiomatic: Then everything went horribly wrong.

sentences.zh: 夫僕與李陵俱居門下，素非相善也，趣捨異路，未嘗銜杯酒接殷勤之歡。

translations.literal: Your servant and Li Ling both dwelt under the same gate; by nature we were not mutually friendly; our aims and rejections were different roads; we never joined in a cup's wine or received earnest cordiality's joy.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling and I served in the same corps but were never close—different paths, never a convivial drink together.

sentences.zh: 然僕觀其為人自奇士，事親孝，與士信，臨財廉，取予義，分別有讓，恭儉下人，常思奮不顧身以徇國家之急。

translations.literal: Yet your servant observed his conduct as a man—he was himself a strange knight: serving parents filially, with gentlemen trustworthy, facing wealth incorrupt, in taking and giving righteous, in distinctions yielding, respectful and frugal and humbling himself below others, always thinking to rouse himself without sparing body to follow the state's urgent need.
translations.idiomatic: Yet I saw him as a true knight: filial, trustworthy, honest with money, fair in dealings, modest, humble, ready to spend himself for the state.

sentences.zh: 其素所畜積也，僕以為有國士之風。

translations.literal: What he had long stored up—your servant deemed he had a state knight's bearing.
translations.idiomatic: That was the man he had long been—I thought him worthy of the title "champion of the realm."

sentences.zh: 夫人臣出萬死不顧一生之計，趙公家之難，斯已奇矣。

translations.literal: As for a minister who goes forth on a plan of ten thousand deaths without regard to one life, hastening to the lordly house's crisis—this is already strange.
translations.idiomatic: A subject who risks annihilation for the state's peril is rare enough.

sentences.zh: 今舉事壹不當，而全軀保妻子之臣隨而媒孽其短，僕誠私心痛之！

translations.literal: Now one undertaking once misses the mark, and whole-body wife-and-child-preserving ministers follow and brew his faults—your servant truly privately is pained at heart!
translations.idiomatic: One misstep, and men who care only for their own skins brew slander against him—it breaks my heart.

sentences.zh: 且李陵提步卒不滿五千，深踐戎馬之地，足歷王庭，垂餌虎口，橫挑強胡，卬億萬之師，與單于連戰十餘日，所殺過當。

translations.literal: Moreover Li Ling led foot soldiers not filling five thousand, deeply trod the horse-and-armor land, foot traversed the royal court, hung bait in the tiger's mouth, recklessly challenged the mighty Hu, faced myriads of millions of troops, fought linked battles with the chanyu over ten-some days, those killed exceeded proportion.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling took fewer than five thousand foot deep into nomad country, dangled bait in the tiger's maw, defied a host of millions, and fought the chanyu day after day, killing more than his numbers would suggest.

sentences.zh: 虜救死扶傷不給，旃裘之君長咸震怖，乃悉征左右賢王，舉引弓之民，一國共攻而圍之。

translations.literal: The captives could not supply aid to the wounded; felt-cloak lords and chiefs all trembled in terror; thereupon they fully summoned left and right wise kings, raised all bow-drawing peoples, the whole state together attacked and surrounded them.
translations.idiomatic: The enemy could not even tend their wounded; felt-clad chiefs panicked and called every wise king and bowman in the realm to ring Han's tiny force.

sentences.zh: 轉鬥千里，矢盡道窮，救兵不至，士卒死傷如積。

translations.literal: They turned and fought a thousand li; arrows were exhausted, roads cut off; relief troops did not arrive; soldiers' dead and wounded piled like heaps.
translations.idiomatic: They fought a thousand li until arrows were gone and the road ended; no help came, and the dead piled high.

sentences.zh: 然李陵一呼勞軍，士無不起，躬流涕，沫血飲泣，張空，冒白刃，北首爭死敵。

translations.literal: Yet Li Ling one call to comfort the army—soldiers without exception rose; personally weeping, foam-blood swallowing sobs, drawing empty [crossbows], braving white blades, north-facing vied to die on the foe.
translations.idiomatic: Yet when Li Ling rallied them, every man rose weeping, wiping blood and tears, drawing empty crossbows, rushing bare blades toward the north to die on the enemy.

sentences.zh: 陵未沒時，使有來報，漢公卿王侯皆奉觴上壽。

translations.literal: Before Ling had not yet sunk, envoys came reporting; Han's dukes, ministers, and marquises all raised cups and offered longevity.
translations.idiomatic: While news still favored him, every noble in Han raised a cup to toast victory.

sentences.zh: 後數日，陵敗書聞，主上為之食不甘味，聽朝不怡。

translations.literal: After several days, Ling's defeat letter was heard; the sovereign for this ate without tasting sweetness, heard court unhappily.
translations.idiomatic: Days later, when defeat was known, the emperor lost his appetite and could not face court with ease.

sentences.zh: 大臣憂懼，不知所出。

translations.literal: Great ministers were anxious and afraid, not knowing what to produce.
translations.idiomatic: Ministers cowered, at a loss for counsel.

sentences.zh: 僕竊不自料其卑賤，見主上慘淒怛悼，誠欲效其款款之愚。

translations.literal: Your servant privately did not measure his base and vile; seeing the sovereign bitter, mournful, and grieved, truly wished to display his earnest foolishness.
translations.idiomatic: I forgot my low station, saw the emperor's grief, and meant only to offer my clumsy loyalty.

sentences.zh: 以為李陵素與士大夫絕甘分少，能得人之死力，雖古名將不過也。

translations.literal: I deemed Li Ling by nature with gentlemen-officers cut off sweetness and shared the little, could obtain men's death-exerting force—even ancient famed generals did not surpass this.
translations.idiomatic: I said Li Ling always denied himself to share with his men and could call forth their last breath—no ancient general did more.

sentences.zh: 身雖陷敗，彼觀其意，且欲得其當而報漢。

translations.literal: Though personally trapped and defeated, observing his intent, he moreover wished to obtain the fit occasion and repay Han.
translations.idiomatic: Though beaten, I believed he still sought the right moment to repay Han.

sentences.zh: 事已無可奈何，其所摧敗，攻亦足以暴於天下。

translations.literal: The affair already had no way to be helped; what he smashed and defeated in attack was also enough to display to all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: The worst had happened, yet the damage he had done the enemy was enough to show the world.

sentences.zh: 僕懷欲陳之，而未有路，適會召問，即以此指推言陵功，欲以廣主上之意，塞睚眥之辭。

translations.literal: Your servant harbored wishing to set it forth, yet had no road; it happened I was summoned and questioned; thereupon by this gist I extended speech of Ling's merit, wishing thereby to broaden the sovereign's mind, stop narrow-eyed men's words.
translations.idiomatic: I had no opening until the summons; then I praised Li Ling's deeds to lift the emperor's mood and silence petty slander.

sentences.zh: 未能盡明，明主不深曉，以為僕沮貳師，而為李陵遊說，遂下於理。

translations.literal: I could not fully clarify; the enlightened lord did not deeply understand, deemed your servant was slandering Ershi and was lobbying for Li Ling, thereupon sent down to the judge.
translations.idiomatic: I spoke unclearly; the emperor thought I maligned Ershi and pleaded for Li Ling, and handed me to the law.

sentences.zh: 拳拳之忠，終不能自列。

translations.literal: Clenched loyalty in the end could not clear itself.
translations.idiomatic: My earnest loyalty never got a hearing.

sentences.zh: 因為誣上，卒從吏議。

translations.literal: Thereupon deemed to have deceived the sovereign above, in the end followed the clerks' judgment.
translations.idiomatic: I was judged to have deceived the emperor and was sentenced as the officials advised.

sentences.zh: 家貧，財賂不足以自贖，交遊莫救，左右親近不為一言。

translations.literal: The family was poor; goods and bribes were insufficient to ransom myself; friends did not save; those close at left and right spoke not one word.
translations.idiomatic: I was too poor to buy off the sentence; friends dared not help, and those nearest me never spoke.

sentences.zh: 身非木石，獨與法吏為伍，深幽囹圄之中，誰可告訴者！

translations.literal: The body is not wood and stone; alone with law clerks as companions, deeply hidden within prison walls—whom can one tell!
translations.idiomatic: I am flesh, not wood or stone, yet I rot alone with jailers in the dark—whom can I tell?

sentences.zh: 此正少卿所親見，僕行事豈不然邪？

translations.literal: This is just what Sir Shaoqing personally saw—your servant's conduct was it not thus?
translations.idiomatic: You saw it yourself, Sir—was it not exactly so?

sentences.zh: 李陵既生降，頹其家聲，而僕又茸以蠶室，重為天下觀笑。

translations.literal: Li Ling having then surrendered alive, toppled his house's fame, while your servant again was bedded in the silkworm chamber, doubly became all under heaven's spectacle and laughter.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling's live surrender shamed his line; I was then sealed in the silkworm chamber—twice over a laughingstock to the world.

sentences.zh: 悲夫！

translations.literal: How sad!
translations.idiomatic: How bitter!

sentences.zh: 悲夫！

translations.literal: How sad!
translations.idiomatic: How bitter indeed!

sentences.zh: 事未易一二為俗人言也。

translations.literal: The affair is not easy to tell in one or two points to vulgar men.
translations.idiomatic: The full story cannot be told in a line or two to the vulgar.

sentences.zh: 僕之先人，非有剖符丹書之功，文史、星曆，近乎卜祝之間，固主上所戲弄，倡優畜之，流俗之所輕也。

translations.literal: Your servant's forbears had no merit of split tallies and cinnabar writs; letters and histories, stars and calendars—close to between diviners and invokers; solidly what the sovereign toys with, keeps like actors and jesters, what vulgar custom despises.
translations.idiomatic: My forebears won no iron-clad honors; they kept archives and stars—little better than court diviners—pets of the throne, kept like actors, despised by the crowd.

sentences.zh: 假令僕伏法受誅，若九牛亡一毛，與螻蟻何異！

translations.literal: Suppose your servant submitted to law and received execution—like one hair lost from nine oxen—how different from ants!
translations.idiomatic: Had I died under the law, I would have been one hair from nine oxen—no more than an ant.

sentences.zh: 而世又不與能死節者比，特以為智窮罪極，不能自免，卒就死耳。

translations.literal: Yet the age again does not rank with those able to die for principle; they only deem wisdom exhausted and crime utmost, unable to save themselves, in the end went to death, that is all.
translations.idiomatic: Yet the world would not call me a martyr—only a fool cornered by crime who could not escape death.

sentences.zh: 何也？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 素所自樹立使然。

translations.literal: What they have by nature set up themselves makes it so.
translations.idiomatic: Because of the station I had chosen.

sentences.zh: 人固有一死，死有重於泰山，或輕於鴻毛，用之所趨異也。

translations.literal: Men indeed have one death; death has that which is heavier than Mount Tai, or lighter than a goose feather—by the use to which it is directed it differs.
translations.idiomatic: All men die once; death may weigh more than Mount Tai or less than a feather—depending on what it serves.

sentences.zh: 太上不辱先，其次不辱身，其次不辱理色，其次不辱辭令，其次詘體受辱，其次易服受辱，其次關木索被箠楚受辱，其次剔毛髮嬰金鐵受辱，其次毀肌膚斷支體受辱，最下腐刑，極矣。

translations.literal: The highest is not shaming forbears; next not shaming the body; next not shaming in principle and countenance; next not shaming in words and commands; next bending the body and receiving shame; next changing clothes and receiving shame; next being bound with cudgel-ropes and receiving flog-cane shame; next shaving hair and winding metal-iron shame; next destroying flesh and cutting limbs shame; lowest is rotten punishment—this is the extreme.
translations.idiomatic: The noblest shame is none to ancestors; then none to oneself; then none in face or bearing; then none in speech; then comes bent body, changed dress, fetters and flogging, shaved head and iron collar, mutilation—and lowest of all castration, the end of shame.

sentences.zh: 傳曰「刑不上大夫」，此言士節不可不厲也。

translations.literal: The tradition says "punishment does not ascend to grandees"—this speaks of gentlemen's integrity, it cannot but be sharpened.
translations.idiomatic: The classic says "the lash does not touch grandees"—meaning a gentleman's honor must be guarded.

sentences.zh: 猛虎處深山，百獸震恐，及其在阱檻之中，搖尾而求食，積威約之漸也。

translations.literal: A fierce tiger dwells in deep mountains—the hundred beasts tremble in terror; when it reaches pit and cage within, it wags tail and seeks food—this is the gradual effect of piled awe and restraint.
translations.idiomatic: A tiger in the hills terrifies every beast; caged, it wags its tail for scraps—awe wears it down by degrees.

sentences.zh: 故士有畫地為牢勢不入，削木為吏議不對，定計於鮮也。

translations.literal: Therefore knights have drawn earth for a prison—by momentum they do not enter; carved wood for a clerk—they do not answer in deliberation—they fix the plan while fresh.
translations.idiomatic: Hence a gentleman would not step into a ring drawn as a jail nor answer a wooden judge—he ends his life while honor is still fresh.

sentences.zh: 今交手足，受木索，暴肌膚，受榜箠，幽於圜牆之中，當此之時，見獄吏則頭槍地，視徒隸則心惕息。

translations.literal: Now hands and feet bound, receiving wood ropes, exposing flesh, receiving flog-bamboo, hidden within round walls—at this time, seeing the jail clerk then head butts earth; seeing convict underlings then heart quails and breath stops.
translations.idiomatic: Now bound hand and foot, stripped and flogged in the round cell, a man kowtows to the turnkey and trembles at convict guards.

sentences.zh: 何者？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 積威約之勢也。

translations.literal: It is the momentum of piled awe and restraint.
translations.idiomatic: Because crushing fear has done its work.

sentences.zh: 及已至此，言不辱者，所謂強顏耳，曷足貴乎！

translations.literal: When already come to this, speaking of not being shamed—so-called forced face only—how is it worth esteem!
translations.idiomatic: At that point to claim you are unshamed is only brazen face—what honor is left?

sentences.zh: 且西伯，伯也，拘牖裡；

translations.literal: Moreover the Western Earl was a lord, yet was detained at Youli;
translations.idiomatic: The Earl of the West was a lord, yet sat in chains at Youli;

sentences.zh: 李斯，相也，具五刑；

translations.literal: Li Si was a chancellor, yet underwent the five punishments;
translations.idiomatic: Li Si was chancellor, yet suffered the five mutilations;

sentences.zh: 淮陰，王也，受械於陳；

translations.literal: The Huaiyin marquis was a king, yet received fetters at Chen;
translations.idiomatic: The king of Huaiyin wore fetters in Chen;

sentences.zh: 彭越、張敖，南鄉稱孤，繫獄具罪；

translations.literal: Peng Yue and Zhang Ao, facing south called themselves orphaned, were bound in prison with crimes complete;
translations.idiomatic: Peng Yue and Zhang Ao had faced south as kings, then landed in jail condemned;

sentences.zh: 絳侯誅諸呂，權傾五伯，囚於請室；

translations.literal: Marquis of Jiang slew the Lüs, power tilted the five hegemons, was imprisoned in the request chamber;
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Jiang, who purged the Lü clan and overshadowed the Five Hegemons, was locked in the plea cell;

sentences.zh: 魏其，大將也，衣赭，關三木；

translations.literal: Dou Ying was a great general, yet wore cinnabar, was locked with three woods;
translations.idiomatic: Dou Ying the general wore convict russet and the triple stocks;

sentences.zh: 季布為硃家鉗奴；

translations.literal: Ji Bu became the Zhu family's collared slave;
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu sold himself collared to the Zhu house;

sentences.zh: 灌夫受辱居室；

translations.literal: Guan Fu received shame in the chamber house;
translations.idiomatic: Guan Fu was shamed under house arrest;

sentences.zh: 此人皆身至王侯將相，聲聞鄰國，及罪至罔加，不能引決自財。

translations.literal: These men all personally reached king, marquis, general, minister, fame heard in neighboring states; when crime arrived and the net applied, could not draw the resolve and end themselves (text: 財 for 裁).
translations.idiomatic: These were kings, generals, and ministers whose names crossed borders—yet when the law closed they could not choose an honorable death.

sentences.zh: 在塵埃之中，古今一體，安在其不辱也！

translations.literal: Within dust and mire, ancient and modern are one body—where is their not being shamed!
translations.idiomatic: In the dust, past and present are the same—where is the shameless man?

sentences.zh: 由此言之，勇怯，勢也；

translations.literal: From this speaking, bravery and cowardice are circumstance;
translations.idiomatic: So courage and fear are matters of circumstance;

sentences.zh: 強弱，形也。

translations.literal: strength and weakness are form.
translations.idiomatic: strength and weakness follow the situation.

sentences.zh: 審矣，曷足怪乎！

translations.literal: This is clear—how is it worth marvel!
translations.idiomatic: Clear enough—why wonder?

sentences.zh: 且人不能蚤自財繩墨之外，已稍陵夷至於鞭箠之間，乃欲引節，斯不亦遠乎！

translations.literal: Moreover men cannot early end themselves outside the ink-line; already gradually declining until reaching between whip and bamboo, then wishing to draw integrity—is this not also far!
translations.idiomatic: If a man does not cut his throat before the law takes him, but slides step by step to the flogging post, then talks of honor—is that not far too late?

sentences.zh: 古人所以重施刑於大夫者，殆為此也。

translations.literal: The reason the ancients stressed applying punishment to grandees was roughly this.
translations.idiomatic: That is why the ancients hesitated to punish high ministers.

sentences.zh: 夫人情莫不貪生惡死，念親戚，顧妻子，至激於義理者不然，乃有不得已也。

translations.literal: As for human feeling, none does not cling to life and hate death, think on kin, look to wife and children—those stirred to utmost by duty and principle are not so; then there is what cannot be helped.
translations.idiomatic: Every man clings to life and loves kin—except when duty leaves no choice.

sentences.zh: 今僕不幸，蚤失二親，無兄弟之親，獨身孤立，少卿視僕於妻子何如哉？

translations.literal: Now your servant is unlucky, early lost both parents, has no brother kin, stands alone solitary—Sir Shaoqing, how do you view your servant regarding wife and children?
translations.idiomatic: I lost parents young, have no brothers, stand alone—what tie have I to wife and child, Sir?

sentences.zh: 且勇者不必死節，怯夫慕義，何處不勉焉！

translations.literal: Moreover the brave need not die for principle; cowards who admire duty—where do they not exert themselves!
translations.idiomatic: The brave need not die for honor; even a coward who loves right can find courage somewhere.

sentences.zh: 僕雖怯耎欲苟活，亦頗識去就之分矣，何至自湛溺累紲之辱哉！

translations.literal: Your servant though timid and weak wishes to cling to life, yet also somewhat knows leaving and taking's division—how reach to sink oneself and drown in piled-fetters shame!
translations.idiomatic: I am a coward who clings to life, yet I know the line between honor and disgrace—why would I willingly wallow in chains and shame?

sentences.zh: 且夫臧獲婢妾猶能引決，況若僕之不得已乎！

translations.literal: Moreover slaves and bondmaids still can draw resolve—how much more like your servant's having no choice!
translations.idiomatic: Even a slave girl can choose death—need I speak of a man driven as I was?

sentences.zh: 所以隱忍苟活，函糞土之中而不辭者，恨私心有所不盡，鄙沒世而文采不表於後也。

translations.literal: The reason I bore shame and clung to life, encased in muck and earth yet did not decline, was hating that private intent was not exhausted, despising dying age while patterned brilliance was not displayed to posterity.
translations.idiomatic: I swallowed shame and lived in filth because my work was unfinished—I could not die leaving my writing unknown.

sentences.zh: 古者富貴而名摩滅，不可勝記，唯俶儻非常之人稱焉。

translations.literal: Of old the rich and noble whose names rubbed out cannot be fully recorded—only those standing apart and extraordinary are praised.
translations.idiomatic: Countless rich men of old are forgotten—only the singular are remembered.

sentences.zh: 蓋西伯拘而演《周易》；

translations.literal: The Western Earl was detained and elaborated the Zhou Changes;
translations.idiomatic: The Earl of the West was jailed and wrote the Changes;

sentences.zh: 仲尼厄而作《春秋》；

translations.literal: Zhongni was in straits and composed the Spring and Autumn;
translations.idiomatic: Confucius in peril wrote the Spring and Autumn;

sentences.zh: 屈原放逐，乃賦《離騷》；

translations.literal: Qu Yuan was banished and thereupon rhapsodized "Encountering Sorrow";
translations.idiomatic: Qu Yuan in exile gave us the Li sao;

sentences.zh: 左丘失明，厥有《國語》，孫子臏腳，《兵法》修列；

translations.literal: Zuo Qiu lost sight, thereupon had the Discourses of the States; Sunzi had feet cut off, the Art of War was ordered;
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Qiu blind gave us the Discourses of the States; Sun Bin maimed, the Art of War was set in order;

sentences.zh: 不韋遷蜀，世傳《呂覽》；

translations.literal: Buwei was removed to Shu, the age transmits the Lü's Examinations;
translations.idiomatic: Lü Buwei banished to Shu left the Lüshi chunqiu;

sentences.zh: 韓非囚秦，《說難》、《孤憤》。

translations.literal: Han Fei was imprisoned in Qin—"Difficulties of Persuasion" and "Solitary Indignation."
translations.idiomatic: Han Fei, a prisoner in Qin, wrote "Difficulties of Persuasion" and "Solitary Indignation."

sentences.zh: 《詩》三百篇，大氐賢聖發憤之所為作也。

translations.literal: The Odes' three hundred pieces—greatly are they what worthies and sages made in venting indignation.
translations.idiomatic: The three hundred odes are mostly the work of sages pouring out rage.

sentences.zh: 此人皆意有所鬱結，不得通其道，故述往事，思來者。

translations.literal: These men all had intent knotted within, could not penetrate their way; therefore they narrated past affairs, thought on those to come.
translations.idiomatic: Each had a knot in the heart and no outlet—so they told the past to reach the future.

sentences.zh: 及如左丘無目，孫子斷足，終不可用，退論書策以舒其憤，思垂空文以自見。

translations.literal: As for like Zuo Qiu without eyes, Sunzi with severed feet, in the end unusable, withdrew to discuss documents and slips to unburden their indignation, thought to hang empty text to display themselves.
translations.idiomatic: Blind Zuo Qiu and footless Sun Bin, cast aside, wrote to vent their rage and leave a voice on the page.

sentences.zh: 僕竊不遜，近自托於無能之辭，網羅天下放失舊聞，考之行事，稽其成敗興壞之理，凡百三十篇，亦欲以究天人之際，通古今之變，成一家之言。

translations.literal: Your servant immodestly has lately entrusted himself to incapable phrasing, netted all under heaven's scattered and lost old hearsay, examined them in deeds and conduct, traced their success-failure rise-ruin pattern—in all one hundred thirty sections—also wishing thereby to exhaust heaven and man's juncture, penetrate ancient and modern's changes, complete one family's words.
translations.idiomatic: I have dared to stitch lost lore into a hundred thirty chapters, testing deeds for why states rise and fall, to trace heaven and man, link past to present, and speak in one voice.

sentences.zh: 草創未就，適會此禍，惜其不成，是以就極刑而無慍色。

translations.literal: Rough draft not yet finished, it happened this disaster met; regretting its not being completed, therefore submitted to utmost punishment without resentful color.
translations.idiomatic: The draft was unfinished when this blow fell; I went to the worst punishment without a flicker of resentment, for fear the book would die with me.

sentences.zh: 僕誠已著此書，藏之名山，傳之其人，通邑大都，則僕償前辱之責，雖萬被戮，豈有悔哉！

translations.literal: If your servant truly completes this book, stores it on a famous mountain, transmits it to the right man, spreads it in great metropolises, then your servant repays the former shame's debt—though ten thousand times cut down, how could there be regret!
translations.idiomatic: If I finish this book, lodge it on a sacred peak, pass it to the right hands, and let it reach the great cities, I will have paid my debt for past shame—then let me die ten thousand deaths without regret.

sentences.zh: 然此可為智者道，難為俗人言也。

translations.literal: Yet this can be told to the wise; it is hard to speak to vulgar men.
translations.idiomatic: That is for the wise alone; the vulgar will never understand.

sentences.zh: 且負下未易居，下流多謗議。

translations.literal: Moreover bearing low station is not easy to dwell in; downstream there is much slander and discussion.
translations.idiomatic: A fallen man cannot live at ease; the world downstream heaps gossip on him.

sentences.zh: 僕以口語遇遭此禍，重為鄉黨戮笑，污辱先人，亦何面目復上父母之丘墓乎？

translations.literal: Your servant by spoken words met this disaster, doubly became village and district's slaughter-laughter, defiled and shamed forbears—what face again to ascend father and mother's mound tombs?
translations.idiomatic: A few words brought this ruin; my neighbors point and laugh; I have shamed my ancestors—how dare I climb my parents' hill again?

sentences.zh: 雖累百世，垢彌甚耳！

translations.literal: Though piled a hundred generations, the stain grows only greater!
translations.idiomatic: The stain will grow darker for a hundred generations.

sentences.zh: 是以腸一日而九迴，居則忽忽若有所亡，出則不知所如往。

translations.literal: Therefore my guts in one day turn nine times; at rest then dazed as if something lost; going out then not knowing where to go.
translations.idiomatic: My bowels twist nine times a day; at home I am lost in a fog; abroad I wander without aim.

sentences.zh: 每念斯恥，汗未嘗不發背沾衣也。

translations.literal: Each time I think on this shame, sweat never fails to break on my back and soak my clothes.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever I remember that shame, sweat runs down my spine and soaks my robe.

sentences.zh: 身直為閨閣之臣，寧得自引深臧於巖穴邪！

translations.literal: The body is straight as a boudoir chamber's minister—how can one draw oneself to hide deep in cliff caves!
translations.idiomatic: I am only a chamber servant now—how can I withdraw deep into the hills?

sentences.zh: 故且從俗浮湛，與時俯仰，以通其狂惑。

translations.literal: Therefore for the moment following vulgarity floating and sinking, with the times bowing and rising, thereby to vent this mad confusion.
translations.idiomatic: So I drift with the crowd, bob with the times, to ease this madness in my breast.

sentences.zh: 今少卿乃教以推賢進士，無乃與僕之私指謬乎？

translations.literal: Now Sir Shaoqing then teaches me to recommend worthies and advance knights—is this not perhaps at odds with your servant's private intent?
translations.idiomatic: Now you urge me to recommend talent—is that not the opposite of what I intend?

sentences.zh: 今雖欲自雕瑑，曼辭以自解，無益，於俗不信，只取辱耳。

translations.literal: Now though wishing to carve and polish oneself, smooth words to excuse oneself—no benefit; to vulgarity not believed, only draws shame, that is all.
translations.idiomatic: Even if I polished fine phrases to clear myself, the crowd would not believe—only more shame.

sentences.zh: 要之死日，然後是非乃定。

translations.literal: In sum, on the day of death, then right and wrong are fixed.
translations.idiomatic: Only when I am dead will judgment settle.

sentences.zh: 書不能盡意，故略陳固陋。

translations.literal: Writing cannot exhaust intent; therefore I briefly set forth my obstinate ignorance.
translations.idiomatic: Ink cannot say it all; I have set out my rough thoughts as best I could.

sentences.zh: 遷既死後，其書稍出。

translations.literal: After Qian had died, his book gradually emerged.
translations.idiomatic: After Sima Qian died, his work slowly reached the world.

sentences.zh: 宣帝時，遷外孫平通侯楊惲祖述其書，遂宣佈焉。

translations.literal: In Emperor Xuan's time, Qian's maternal grandson Marquis of Pingtong Yang Yun first narrated his book, thereupon proclaimed it.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan, his grandson Yang Yun published and spread the text.

sentences.zh: 王莽時，求封遷後，為史通子。

translations.literal: In Wang Mang's time, he sought to enfeoff Qian's posterity, making them Master of Historical Communication.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang sought out Qian's heirs and enfeoffed them as "Master of Historical Communication."

sentences.zh: 贊曰：自古書契之作而有史官，其載籍愽矣。

translations.literal: The eulogium says: From antiquity when writing and tallying were made, there have been scribes; their loaded records are vast.
translations.idiomatic: The summation reads: Since writing began there have been historians, and their archives run deep.

sentences.zh: 至孔氏籑之，上繼唐堯，下訖秦繆。

translations.literal: Down to Confucius's clan compiling them, upward continuing Tang Yao, downward ending at Qin Mu.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius edited them from Yao of Tang down to Duke Mu of Qin.

sentences.zh: 唐虞以前雖有遺文，其語不經，故言黃帝、顓頊之事未可明也。

translations.literal: Before Tang and Yu although there were stray documents, their language was not canonical; therefore speaking of the Yellow Thearch and Zhuanxu's affairs cannot be made clear.
translations.idiomatic: Before Yao and Shun, fragments survive but are not canonical—so tales of the Yellow Thearch and Zhuanxu cannot be trusted as history.

sentences.zh: 及孔子因魯史記而作《春秋》，而左丘明論輯其本事是以為之傳，又籑異同為《國語》。

translations.literal: When Confucius by Lu's scribal records composed the Spring and Autumn, and Zuo Qiuming discussed and compiled its root affairs thereby making its commentary, again compiled differences and samenesses into the Discourses of the States.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn from Lu's annals; Zuo Qiuming wrote its commentary and wove variant traditions into the Discourses of the States.

sentences.zh: 又有世本，錄黃帝以來至春秋時帝王公侯卿大夫祖世所出。

translations.literal: Moreover there was the Genealogical Origins, recording from the Yellow Thearch down to Spring and Autumn times' emperors, kings, dukes, marquises, ministers, and great officers' ancestral generations' emergence.
translations.idiomatic: The Shiben recorded royal and noble lines from the Yellow Thearch through the Spring and Autumn age.

sentences.zh: 春秋之後，七國並爭，秦兼諸侯，有《戰國策》。

translations.literal: After the Spring and Autumn, seven states strove together; Qin annexed the feudal lords—there was the Intrigues of the Warring States.
translations.idiomatic: After the Spring and Autumn came the warring kingdoms, and the Intrigues of the Warring States records Qin's swallowing of the lords.

sentences.zh: 漢興伐秦定天下，有《楚漢春秋》。

translations.literal: Han rose, attacked Qin, settled all under heaven—there was the Chu-Han Spring and Autumn.
translations.idiomatic: When Han rose against Qin, the Chu-Han Spring and Autumn chronicled the conquest.

sentences.zh: 故司馬遷據《左氏》、《國語》，采《世本》、《戰國策》，述《楚漢春秋》，接其後事，訖于大漢。

translations.literal: Therefore Sima Qian relied on the Zuo tradition and Discourses of the States, gathered Genealogical Origins and Intrigues of the Warring States, narrated the Chu-Han Spring and Autumn, linked their later affairs, ending at great Han.
translations.idiomatic: Sima Qian drew on Zuo, the Discourses, the Shiben, the Intrigues, and the Chu-Han Spring and Autumn, then carried the story down to his own Han.

sentences.zh: 其言秦漢，詳矣。

translations.literal: His speaking of Qin and Han is detailed.
translations.idiomatic: His account of Qin and Han is full and precise.

sentences.zh: 至於采經摭傳，分散數家之事，甚多疏略，或有抵梧。

translations.literal: As for gathering classics and plucking commentaries, scattering several houses' affairs—very much is loose and omitted, or has contradiction.
translations.idiomatic: In weaving classics and traditions from many schools he often skims or leaves gaps, and sometimes contradicts himself.

sentences.zh: 亦其涉獵者廣博，貫穿經傳，馳騁古今，上下數千載間，斯以勤矣。

translations.literal: Also his ranging was broad and vast; he pierced classics and commentaries, galloped ancient and modern, up and down several thousand years within—this is diligence.
translations.idiomatic: Yet his reading was vast, he threaded classics and commentary across thousands of years—an immense labor.

sentences.zh: 又其是非頗繆於聖人，論大道則先黃老而後六經，序遊俠則退處士而進姦雄，述貨殖則崇埶利而羞賤貧，此其所蔽也。

translations.literal: Moreover his right and wrong often errs from the sages; discussing the great way he puts Huang-Lao first and the six classics after; ordering wandering knights he demotes recluses and advances villain-heroes; narrating commodity producers he esteems power-profit and shames base poverty—this is where he is blinded.
translations.idiomatic: His judgments sometimes stray from the sages: he ranks Huang-Lao above the six classics, favors adventurers over true recluses, and glorifies wealth while slighting the poor—those are his blind spots.

sentences.zh: 然自劉向、楊雄博極羣書，皆稱遷有良史之材，服其善序事理，辨而不華，質而不俚，其文直，其事核，不虛美，不隱惡，故謂之實錄。

translations.literal: Yet from Liu Xiang and Yang Xiong, who exhausted the host of books, all praised Qian as having a good scribe's talent, submitted to his good ordering of affairs' pattern, discriminating yet not flowery, plain yet not vulgar, his text straight, his affairs verified, not empty in praise, not hiding evil—therefore it is called a true record.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Liu Xiang and Yang Xiong, who read everything, called him a born historian: clear in narrative, plain but not crude, straight in prose and tight in fact, neither flattering nor concealing—hence the title "true record."

sentences.zh: 烏呼！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 以遷之博物洽聞，而不能以知自全，旣陷極刑，幽而發憤，書亦信矣。

translations.literal: With Qian's broad things and comprehensive hearing, yet unable by knowledge to preserve himself; already sunk in utmost punishment, hidden yet venting indignation—the book is also trustworthy.
translations.idiomatic: So learned a man could not save himself with wit; cast down to the mutilating stroke, he wrote from the dark in rage—and the book rings true.

sentences.zh: 迹其所以自傷悼，小雅巷伯之倫。

translations.literal: Tracing the reason he thus wounded and mourned himself—of the Lesser Odes' "Alley Steward" class.
translations.idiomatic: The tone of his self-lament belongs with the "Alley Steward" in the Lesser Odes.

sentences.zh: 夫唯大雅「旣明且哲，能保其身」，難矣哉！

translations.literal: As for only the Greater Odes' "already bright and wise, able to preserve his person"—hard indeed!
translations.idiomatic: Only the Greater Odes' "clear-sighted and wise enough to save his own skin"—that is rare indeed!
